entries.desc: Analyzes Wellbeing Engagement Collection (WEC) index data from over 215,000 students in Years 4â€“10 across South Australia (2016â€“2019) to examine the relationship between wellbeing and academic outcomes. Learning readiness emerged as the most influential wellbeing component, with a more pronounced influence on male students particularly in literacy; effects were stable across year levels and time periods. The findings demonstrate learning analytics' potential for deepening system-level understanding of socio-emotional factors' relationship to academic success.

entries.title: Learning Analytics in Schools: Is Digital Data Use Influenced by Teacher-Level or School-Level Factors?
entries.desc: Surveys 2,247 teachers across 112 Swiss upper secondary schools to investigate what teacher-level and school-level factors drive educators' use of digital data for instructional decisions. Teacher-specific attributes â€” technology confidence, positive attitudes toward digital tools, and data access â€” significantly predict digital data use, while school-level factors such as collegial collaboration and principal support operated indirectly through individual teacher characteristics. The authors suggest findings can guide customized professional development strategies aligned with educator strengths and supportive institutional conditions.

entries.title: Targeted School-Based Interventions for Improving Reading and Mathematics for Students with or at Risk of Academic Difficulties in Grades Kâ€“6
entries.desc: This 2021 Campbell systematic review synthesized 205 high-quality studies (607 total) examining school-based interventions for students with or at risk of academic difficulties in grades Kâ€“6. Peer-assisted instruction and small-group adult instruction showed the largest improvements in both reading and mathematics. Effects were positive in the short term with some fadeout but detectable gains up to two years post-intervention.

entries.title: Targeted School-Based Interventions for Improving Reading and Mathematics for Students with or at Risk of Academic Difficulties in Grades 7â€“12
entries.desc: This 2020 Campbell systematic review examined 71 studies (52 RCTs) of school-based interventions for at-risk students in grades 7â€“12. Average effects on standardized reading and math tests were positive and statistically significant, with mathematics interventions showing larger effects than reading. Small-group instruction substantially outperformed computer-assisted instruction.

entries.title: Mindfulness-Based Interventions for Improving Cognition, Academic Achievement, Behavior, and Socio-Emotional Functioning of Primary and Secondary Students
entries.desc: This 2017 Campbell systematic review synthesized 35 randomized or quasi-experimental studies (6,207 participants) of school-based mindfulness interventions. MBIs produced small, statistically significant positive effects on cognitive and socioemotional outcomes, but not on behavioral or academic outcomes. The high risk of bias across included studies means further high-quality evidence is needed.

entries.title: Effects of Trauma-Informed Approaches in Schools
entries.desc: This 2019 Campbell systematic review searched for studies of trauma-informed approaches in schools â€” programs integrating workforce development, trauma-focused services, and organizational environment changes â€” but found zero studies meeting inclusion criteria. Despite widespread adoption of trauma-informed practices in US and international schools, no rigorous evaluations had been conducted at the time of the review.

entries.title: 21st Century Adaptive Teaching and Individualized Learning Operationalized as Specific Blends of Student-Centered Instructional Events
entries.desc: This 2019 Campbell systematic review analyzed 299 studies (43,175 students, 2000â€“2017) comparing student-centered versus teacher-centered instruction in Kâ€“12. More student-centered conditions produced moderate positive effects on achievement; however, giving students excessive control over pacing showed a significantly negative relationship with outcomes. The combination of adaptability with a guiding teacher role produced the strongest effects.

entries.title: The Relationship Between Homework Time and Academic Performance Among K-12: A Systematic Review
entries.desc: This 2024 Campbell systematic review examined 11 experimental studies on homework effects for Kâ€“12 students. Homework showed a beneficial effect on primary school academic achievement, especially arithmetic computation and problem-solving. However, only one of the included studies was published after 1995, and the optimal duration of homework remains unclear, pointing to an urgent need for contemporary high-quality experiments.

entries.title: Mobile Device Use in the Primary School Classroom and Impact on Pupil Literacy and Numeracy Attainment: A Systematic Review
entries.desc: This 2024 Campbell systematic review examined 18 studies (11,126 participants) of tablets, smartphones, and handheld digital games in primary classrooms for children aged 4â€“12. Children using mobile devices showed better results on maths and literacy tests compared to control conditions, though study quality was mostly moderate-to-high risk of bias. Insufficient evidence exists to determine which device types or teaching strategies are most effective.

entries.title: Montessori Education's Impact on Academic and Nonacademic Outcomes
entries.desc: This 2023 Campbell meta-analysis examined 32 rigorous studies (1970â€“2020) comparing Montessori to traditional education across preschool through high school in eight countries. Students in Montessori settings scored approximately 0.25 SD higher on academic outcomes and 0.33 SD higher on non-academic outcomes (executive function, well-being, social skills, creativity). Effects were strongest at preschool/elementary levels and in randomized studies.

entries.title: Small Class Sizes for Improving Student Achievement in Primary and Secondary Schools
entries.desc: This 2018 Campbell systematic review examined 127 studies from 41 countries (10 in meta-analysis) on class size effects on academic achievement. The review concluded that small class sizes have at best a very small effect on reading and no significant effect on mathematics. Given the high cost of class size reduction and minimal demonstrated benefit, the authors emphasize the need for better evidence before resource allocation.

entries.title: Later School Start Times for Supporting the Education, Health, and Well-Being of High School Students
entries.desc: This 2017 Campbell systematic review examined 17 reports of 11 interventions (297,994 participants across six countries) on delayed high school start times. Later start times appear to increase sleep, and some studies suggest positive associations with academic and psychosocial outcomes, but overall evidence quality is low and insufficient to draw firm conclusions. Possible adverse effects include reduced parent interaction and scheduling difficulties.

entries.title: The Effectiveness of Volunteer Tutoring Programs
entries.desc: This 2006 Campbell systematic review analyzed 28 study cohorts (1,676 participants, grades Kâ€“8) of volunteer tutoring programs in the USA. Programs showed positive effects on reading and language outcomes equivalent to approximately one-third of a standard deviation improvement (overall reading effect size: d = 0.23â€“0.30). Highly structured tutoring programs produced significantly greater effects than less structured approaches; reading comprehension and mathematics showed positive but non-significant trends.

entries.title: Dropout Prevention and Intervention Programs: Effects on School Completion and Dropout Among School-Aged Children and Youth
entries.desc: This 2011 Campbell systematic review examined 548 reports describing 167 studies (1985â€“2010) of school and community-based dropout prevention programs. The review found that school dropout can be reduced through carefully implemented programs, but no single prevention or intervention strategy stood out as better than any other. Implementation quality and program intensity mattered more than the specific program type chosen.

entries.title: Impacts of After-School Programs on Student Outcomes
entries.desc: This 2006 Campbell systematic review examined five experimental studies of after-school programs combining academics with youth development and recreation activities. Programs did not contribute to higher reading achievement (d = 0.028, ns) or significant grade improvements, though they reduced unsupervised self-care by more than 0.5 SD and increased participation in enriching activities. Eighty-four percent of measured impacts showed no significant differences between program and control youth.

entries.title: Approaches to Parent Involvement for Improving the Academic Performance of Elementary School-Aged Children
entries.desc: This 2006 Campbell systematic review found that parent involvement has a positive and significant effect on children's overall academic performance (overall d = 0.45; direct parent involvement programs d = 0.31). Benefits were observed even with a median intervention duration of only 11 weeks. The review provides support for parent involvement as a viable supplementary intervention, backing policies like the No Child Left Behind parent involvement mandate.

entries.title: School-Based Interventions for Reducing Disciplinary School Exclusion
entries.desc: This 2018 Campbell systematic review examined 37 RCTs (mostly US and UK) of school-based interventions targeting exclusion rates in children aged 4â€“18. Interventions caused a small, significant drop in exclusion rates during the first six months, but effects did not sustain long-term, and no impact on antisocial behavior was detected. Academic skills enhancement, counselling, mentoring, and teacher skills training all showed some promise.

entries.title: The 'Tools of the Mind' Curriculum for Improving Self-Regulation in Early Childhood
entries.desc: This 2017 Campbell systematic review evaluated the Tools of the Mind early childhood curriculum across 14 records from 6 US studies. The curriculum showed statistically significant but small improvements in mathematics skills; improvements in self-regulation and literacy were not statistically significant. The evidence base is limited by the small number of included studies and methodological weaknesses, and more rigorous research on self-regulation outcomes is needed.

entries.title: Preschool Predictors of Later Reading Comprehension Ability
entries.desc: This 2017 Campbell systematic review examined 64 longitudinal studies (1986â€“2016) of preschool language skills as predictors of later reading comprehension. A broad set of language skills â€” both code-related (phoneme awareness, letter knowledge) and linguistic comprehension â€” are important for developing reading comprehension. The authors recommend that reading comprehension instruction target a broad set of language skills rather than focusing narrowly on decoding alone.

entries.title: The Relationship Between Teacher Qualification and the Quality of the Early Childhood Care and Learning Environment
entries.desc: This 2017 Campbell systematic review analyzed 48 studies with 82 independent samples on the relationship between teacher qualifications and early childhood education and care (ECEC) quality. Higher teacher qualifications are positively associated with higher overall quality in center-based ECEC settings, including program structure, language development, and reasoning skills. Because the evidence is correlational rather than causal, the authors recommend studies that can establish causal pathways through specific teacher competencies.

entries.title: Indicated Truancy Interventions: Effects on School Attendance Among Chronic Truant Students
entries.desc: This 2012 Campbell systematic review examined 28 studies from the US, Canada, UK, and Australia (1,725 participants; 16 in meta-analysis) of truancy interventions for chronically absent students. Interventions were effective: treated students attended an average of 4.7 more school days than controls. No significant differences in effectiveness were found across different delivery channels or program formats, though attendance remained below acceptable levels in most cases.

entries.title: The Effects of Teachers' Classroom Management Practices on Disruptive or Aggressive Student Behaviour
entries.desc: This 2011 Campbell systematic review examined 12 studies of teacher classroom management practices in US and Dutch Kâ€“12 classrooms. Teachers' classroom management practices had a significant positive effect in decreasing aggressive or problematic student behavior across all included studies. However, the small sample sizes prevent definitive conclusions, and researchers could not identify which specific management components were most effective.

entries.title: Service Learning for Improving Academic Success in Students in Grades K to 12
entries.desc: This 2022 Campbell systematic review examined 10 US-based studies (over 8,000 participants) of school-based service learning â€” programs linking community service to academic instruction â€” in grades Kâ€“12. Evidence regarding academic success was inconclusive due to insufficient comparable studies, and no adverse effects were documented. The review is constrained to US contexts, limiting international generalizability.

entries.title: Psychosocial Interventions for School Refusal with Primary and Secondary School Students
entries.desc: This 2015 Campbell systematic review examined 8 rigorous studies (435 participants, 1980â€“2013) of psychosocial interventions for school refusal in primary and secondary students. Cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) significantly increased school attendance but had no statistically significant effect on anxiety. Findings should be treated with caution due to unclear randomization in most studies and the small evidence base.

entries.title: 'No Excuses' Charter Schools for Increasing Math and Literacy Achievement in Primary and Secondary Education
entries.desc: This 2017 Campbell systematic review examined 18 US studies (5 RCTs, 13 quasi-experimental, 1990â€“2015) of 'No Excuses' charter schools, which combine high academic expectations, strict discipline, extended instruction time, and intensive teacher training. These schools are associated with greater student gains on standardized math and literacy measures than traditional public schools, with notably stronger effects in mathematics that grew over three years. Caution is warranted due to reliance on studies from a single charter management organization (KIPP).

entries.title: Self-Management Interventions for Reducing Challenging Behaviors Among School-Age Students
entries.desc: This 2022 Campbell systematic review analyzed 75 single-case design studies and 4 group-design studies of school-based self-management interventions for students with challenging behaviors. Interventions showed moderate effects for both behavioral and academic outcomes â€” including on-task behavior, prosocial behaviors, and work completion â€” with more meaningful effects for African-American students and students receiving special education services.

entries.title: The Effect of Linguistic Comprehension Instruction on Generalized Language and Reading Comprehension Skills
entries.desc: This 2019 Campbell systematic review analyzed 43 studies (1992â€“2017) of linguistic comprehension interventions in preschool and school-aged children. Results showed a small positive immediate effect on generalized linguistic comprehension (vocabulary and grammar gains were small; narrative and listening comprehension gains were moderate), with negligible effects on reading comprehension. Programs appear insufficient to close vocabulary gaps, and preventing reading difficulties likely requires longer-term educational efforts.

entries.title: Getting state 'free college' right: Design choices that matter
entries.desc: This report examines state-level "free college" promise programs and finds they work best when "generous, easy to access, and paired with dedicated advising support." Although roughly one-third of first-year students attend college tuition-free, living expenses remain a heavy burdenâ€”low-income families spend about 38% of income on non-tuition costs after aid. The authors recommend states shift from last-dollar to first-dollar aid models and plan for federal funding volatility.

entries.title: The complex ramifications of Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard
entries.desc: This analysis documents a "cascade effect" following the 2023 Supreme Court ruling that ended race-conscious college admissions: underrepresented minority students displaced from elite institutions move to state flagships and less-selective schools, reshaping enrollment patterns across higher education. Black student enrollment has declined most sharply at elite colleges, and standardized testing policy significantly mediates diversity outcomes across institution types.

entries.title: Rebuilding IES to amplify the impact of education research on student outcomes
entries.desc: This commentary argues for rebuilding the Institute of Education Sciences following roughly $1 billion in federal budget cuts, contending that "policymakers, practitioners, and education leaders value evidence" and need credible data to guide decisions. The authors propose three federal investment priorities: strengthening researcher-practitioner partnerships, funding implementation and cost studies, and maintaining rigorous, independent, publicly accessible research.

entries.title: How immigration enforcement is harming US schools and students
entries.desc: This article synthesizes research showing that intensified immigration enforcement correlates with increased chronic absenteeism, mental health deterioration, declining test scores, and reduced college enrollment rates among students from immigrant families, with particularly severe effects on Latino and English learner populations. The rescission of sanctuary protections for schools has left educators facing mounting pressures without adequate federal guidance or support systems.

entries.title: Parents are not fully aware of, or concerned about, their children's school attendance
entries.desc: This research finds that fewer than half of caregivers whose children are at risk of chronic absenteeism report being concerned about it, and reveals a significant gap between actual attendance data and parent-reported absences. Many parents view available online learning as offsetting the importance of in-person attendance, despite expressing belief that classroom presence mattersâ€”an attitude that may undermine post-pandemic recovery efforts.

entries.title: School counselors are vital to navigating the FAFSA maze
entries.desc: Examining the troubled rollout of the redesigned FAFSA, this piece presents research showing that adding school counselors significantly increases students' receipt of Pell grants and state scholarships. The author makes three recommendations: prioritize counselor time for FAFSA support, have states extend scholarship deadlines, and push college decision day back to June 1 to accommodate delays.

entries.title: Career and technical education is a hidden weak spot in many high schools' teacher workforces
entries.desc: Using Tennessee data, this research finds that CTE teachers in high-growth occupational fieldsâ€”IT, health sciences, and STEMâ€”leave teaching at significantly higher rates than other educators, with departing instructors earning approximately 20% more in private sector roles. This turnover creates staffing challenges that sometimes result in fewer course offerings for students in career and technical programs.

entries.title: Selective colleges and universities need to do more to enroll low-income students
entries.desc: This study evaluates the Advising Plus program, which used small financial incentives ($50â€“$100) to encourage low-income high school students to engage with remote college advisors. While the intervention successfully increased advising engagement and college applications, it "had no effect on whether or where students enrolled in college," suggesting behavioral nudges alone cannot overcome systemic admissions and affordability barriers at elite institutions.

entries.title: Long-term solutions for college affordability require congressional and state action
entries.desc: Analyzing student debt cancellation and FAFSA implementation, this piece argues that meaningful progress on college affordability requires legislative action from Congress and statesâ€”not executive initiatives operating within limited authority. The author uses both policy cases to demonstrate how legislative inaction forces executive branch workarounds that leave structural affordability problems unresolved.

entries.title: Many high schools across the United States offer limited civics-focused extracurricular activities
entries.desc: Using a nationally representative survey of approximately 650 high school principals, this research finds that while 80% of schools offer student government, other civic activitiesâ€”Model UN (19%), mock trials (26%)â€”remain rare. High-poverty schools provide substantially fewer civics opportunities than low-poverty schools, raising equity concerns about students' access to civic learning experiences that support democratic participation.

entries.title: Early reading tests can aid educational recovery after the pandemic
entries.desc: This study presents Virginia data showing that over one-quarter of kindergarten and first-grade students fell significantly behind in early reading skills in fall 2020â€”a 10-percentage-point increase from the prior year. The authors emphasize evidence-based interventions aligned with the science of reading to address emerging disparities and prevent long-term academic struggles.

entries.title: School finance reforms made funding more equal by income, but not by race
entries.desc: Analyzing state school finance reforms since 1990, this research finds that income-based reforms reduced funding disparities between low- and high-income districts by over $1,300 per pupil but "either did not change or widened funding gaps" between districts by racial composition, increasing racial funding inequalities by $740â€“$1,100 per pupil. The authors argue income-based policies alone cannot address racial disparities without considering school segregation patterns.

entries.title: 2016 Brown Center Report on American Education: How Well Are American Students Learning?
entries.desc: This flagship annual report covers three topics: Common Core implementation effects on math and reading instruction (finding no evidence that Common Core caused 2015 test score declines), whether eighth-grade math tracking correlates with AP success (states with more tracked classes produced more high-performing AP students across racial groups), and international variation in school principal leadership. The analysis concludes that high-performing countries show principals are reluctant to offer direct teaching advice.

entries.title: The impact of COVID-19 on student achievement and what it may mean for educators
entries.desc: This study projects pandemic learning losses using historical test score data from millions of students, estimating that students could return to school in fall 2020 with roughly 70% of their prior-year reading gains and less than 50% of math gains. The authors also project increased variability in academic achievement levels, warning that equity gaps will widen across the student population.

entries.title: The pandemic has had devastating impacts on learning. What will it take to help students catch up?
entries.desc: Measuring fall 2021 test scores for grades 3â€“8, this research found average math scores were 0.20â€“0.27 standard deviations below pre-pandemic levels, with reading also declining significantly. The study evaluates whether common recovery interventionsâ€”tutoring, summer programs, class-size reductionsâ€”are sufficient in scale and evidence base to close pandemic-era learning gaps, noting that equity disparities widened considerably during school closures.

entries.title: A restorative approach to student discipline shows promise in reducing suspensions and arrests
entries.desc: Examining Chicago Public Schools' implementation of restorative practices, this research finds that the program reduced student suspension days by 18% in the four years after rollout, with Black students experiencing double the average reductions. The study provides causal evidence that restorative approachesâ€”emphasizing dialogue and harm reparation over punishmentâ€”can meaningfully reduce disciplinary disparities.

entries.title: Reclassifying English Language Learners: What's the effect on Wisconsin high schoolers?
entries.desc: This study finds that reclassifying English Language Learners as fully proficient during high school increases composite ACT scores by about one full point and boosts postsecondary enrollment, particularly at four-year institutions. The results suggest schools should ensure equitable access to college preparation resources for students scoring near proficiency reclassification thresholds.

entries.title: Do teacher expectations matter?
entries.desc: Analyzing nationally representative data from approximately 6,000 tenth-grade students, this research provides causal evidence that teacher expectations directly affect students' long-term educational attainment. The authors also document that white teachers hold systematically lower expectations for Black students compared to Black teachers, and that this racial bias in expectations has measurable negative effects on Black students' college completion rates.

entries.title: How does student-teacher matching affect suspensions for students of color?
entries.desc: Using 10 years of New York City public school data, this study finds that Black, Latino, and Asian American students experience fewer suspensions when assigned to teachers of the same racial or ethnic background. The authors estimate that increasing teacher diversity could prevent thousands of student suspensions annually and keep students in classrooms for thousands more instructional days over a decade.

entries.title: High school computer science impacts college majors and increases earnings
entries.desc: Using Maryland longitudinal data, this research finds that taking a high school computer science course leads to a five-percentage-point increase in students' likelihood of earning a bachelor's degree in CS and improves employment outcomes. Benefits are particularly pronounced for historically underrepresented groups, though these same students show lower enrollment rates when CS courses become availableâ€”pointing to access and encouragement gaps.

entries.title: Special education: Beneficial to many, harmful to others
entries.desc: Examining a 2005 Texas policy capping special education enrollment, this research finds that students who genuinely need special education services benefit significantly from them, while those inappropriately placed experience worse long-term educational outcomes. Notably, reducing racial disproportionality in placement improved outcomes for Black students, suggesting some were previously misclassified into special education rather than supported through general education.

entries.title: Class size: What research says and what it means for state policy
entries.desc: This review of empirical literature finds that reductions of 7â€“10 fewer students per class can produce meaningful academic gains, particularly for disadvantaged elementary students, but that the magnitude of effect varies considerably across studies and grade levels. The authors conclude that policymakers must weigh class-size reduction against other evidence-based educational investments given the substantial and ongoing fiscal costs involved.

entries.title: Examining Implementation and Outcomes of the Project On-Track High-Dosage Tutoring Program
entries.desc: REL Appalachia (REL 2024-005) examined how northeastern Tennessee school districts implemented the Niswonger Foundation's Project On-Track literacy tutoring initiative for early elementary students. The program uses Amplify Reading to group students by skill level and generate mini-lessons aligned to the science of reading. After one year, 42% of students who started as most at-risk improved to lower risk categories; rural schools heavily favored in-school delivery (92%) compared to non-rural schools (47%).

entries.title: Effectiveness of Early Literacy Instruction: Summary of 20 Years of Research
entries.desc: REL Southeast (REL 2021-084) synthesized over 74,000 studies published between 1997 and 2017, ultimately identifying 132 evidence-based early literacy interventions across 109 high-quality experimental and quasi-experimental studies. The review found that domain-specific instruction reliably increases performance in that domain, and that combined phonological awareness and print knowledge instruction shows promise for improving decoding and early writing skills. Language-focused instruction was found to be more effective in small group or one-on-one settings.

entries.title: Developing a Measure of Middle School Math Teachers' Pedagogical Content Knowledge for Computational Thinking
entries.desc: REL Midwest (REL 2026-007) developed and validated the CT-PCK Survey, a brief instrument measuring middle school teachers' ability to integrate computational thinking into mathematics instruction. Teachers scored approximately 61% correct on average, with those holding traditional certifications and those with five or more years of experience performing significantly higher. The survey is designed for use in professional development evaluation and institutional needs assessment in Midwest urban schools facing declining math achievement.

entries.title: A Pilot Study of Write to Succeed: An Intervention to Enhancing Writing Instruction in New Mexico
entries.desc: REL Southwest (REL 2025-013) evaluated the Write to Succeed professional learning program in partnership with the New Mexico Public Education Department. The pilot involved 22 teachers across five schools in three districts during the 2023-24 school year, with a focus on scaffolded writing instruction that embeds supports for English learner students. Data collected via coach logs, teacher surveys, and interviews provided the New Mexico PED with implementation evidence for potential expansion.

entries.title: California's Special Education Local Plan Areas: Funding Patterns, Inclusion Rates, and Student Outcomes
entries.desc: REL West (REL 2022-134) analyzed 2016-17 data on California's Special Education Local Plan Areas (SELPAs), examining how funding configurations, inclusion rates, and student outcomes vary across SELPA types. The authors recommend that California investigate which regionalized special education programs benefit students in small districts and gather additional data to support more complex analyses of outcomes attributable to specific SELPA configurations.

entries.title: Implementation of Career- and College-Ready Requirements for High School Graduation in Washington
entries.desc: REL Northwest (REL 2020-020) examined Washington state's 2014 legislation requiring 24 credits for high school graduation, finding that district implementation grew from 9% for the class of 2018 to 56% for the class of 2019. Only 27% of 2018 graduates statewide met all requirements, with significant gaps among economically disadvantaged students, English learners, and students of color. Increased credit requirements showed minimal impact on student academic outcomes between 2013 and 2018.

entries.title: Additional Certification for Teachers in New York State: Teachers' Experience and Employment Location, Certification Pathways, and Certification Areas
entries.desc: REL Northeast & Islands (REL 2022-110) studied how New York State teachers with at least one year of experience obtained additional certificates between October 2015 and October 2017. Approximately 5% earned additional certificates during the period; more teachers earned certificates in shortage areas than non-shortage areas, with special education as the most frequently pursued area. Teachers obtaining additional certificates were less experienced on average than those who did not, and over half used traditional in-state certification pathways.

entries.title: Examining the Mentoring Experiences of First-Year Teachers in Missouri
entries.desc: REL Central (REL 2025-014) examined the frequency, duration, activities, and topics of mentoring received by a sample of first-year teachers in Missouri to provide the state Department of Elementary and Secondary Education with data for strengthening its mentoring initiative. The research used descriptive statistics to analyze mentoring patterns, supporting Missouri's goal of improving new teacher retention through evidence-informed program design.

entries.title: Stabilizing School Performance Indicators in New Jersey to Reduce the Effect of Random Error
entries.desc: REL Mid-Atlantic (REL 2025-009) tested Bayesian hierarchical modeling as a method to reduce the impact of random error on school accountability indicators in New Jersey, particularly for smaller student groups. Stabilization substantially improved reliability for test-based indicators such as proficiency rates and median student growth percentiles, and led to meaningful changes in which schools were designated for support and improvement. Non-test-based indicators such as chronic absenteeism and graduation rates proved less amenable to the approach.

entries.title: Examining the English Language Arts Course Placement System at the College of the Marshall Islands
entries.desc: REL Pacific (REL 2026-006) evaluated a 2021-22 revision to the College of the Marshall Islands' ELA placement system that added a multiple-choice test and a writing component alongside high school GPA. Only 11% of first-time students placed into credit-bearing ELA courses under the new system compared to 22% previously; students in intensive pathways of credit-bearing courses showed the highest passing rates. The writing component did not predict any outcomes, leading the authors to recommend CMI consider removing it and weighting high school GPA more heavily.

entries.title: Evidence and Gap Map of Tier 2 Literacy Interventions for Grades Kâ€“3 in the Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands
entries.desc: REL Pacific (REL 2025-007) conducted a systematic review of supplemental literacy programs for Kâ€“3 students in the Northern Mariana Islands, where in 2019 only 24% of grade 3 students were reading at or above grade level. Of 267 relevant studies identified, 20 met rigorous quality standards; two interventions â€” Reading Recovery and Literacy First â€” demonstrated strong evidence under ESSA standards, and Achieve3000 showed moderate evidence. The report includes an evidence gap map and a matrix documenting implementation strategies for each reviewed intervention.

entries.title: Teaching Fractions Toolkit
entries.desc: REL Midwest developed this professional development toolkit in partnership with the Illinois State Board of Education to help grade 6 math teachers implement evidence-based practices for teaching fractions and rational number computation. Grounded in the WWC practice guide Developing Effective Fractions Instruction for Kindergarten Through 8th Grade, the toolkit includes teacher professional development materials, resources for instructional leaders, and supplemental training for state, regional, and district leaders. The initiative aims to strengthen students' conceptual understanding of fractions, ratios, and rates with particular focus on closing achievement gaps.

entries.title: Strategies to Improve Reading
entries.desc: REL Midwest partnered with Michigan school districts to develop and implement the STIR (Strategies to Improve Reading) approach for grades Kâ€“2 literacy instruction, focusing on phonological awareness and phonics practices that help students decode unfamiliar words. The initiative builds educator capacity across three areas: effective instructional practices, data-driven decision-making, and leadership support. Resources, tools, trainings, and coaching are provided to help teachers implement and refine evidence-based early literacy routines.

entries.title: A Toolkit to Support Evidence-Based Writing Instruction in Grades 2 Through 4
entries.desc: REL Mid-Atlantic created this free professional development toolkit comprising eight one-hour professional learning community sessions designed for grades 2â€“4 teachers, school leaders, and PD coaches. Built on the WWC What Works Clearinghouse practice guide on writing instruction and informed by practitioners from four Washington, D.C. charter schools, the toolkit provides a facilitator's guide, teacher's guide, instructional videos, lesson plans, and a school leader's guide. Components include self-reflection tools and goal-setting resources to support ongoing improvement in writing instruction.

entries.title: Toolkit to Support Evidence-Based Algebra Instruction in Middle and High School
entries.desc: REL Central developed this professional development toolkit to help algebra educators implement evidence-based recommendations from the WWC practice guide on improving algebra knowledge in middle and high school students. The toolkit organizes resources around three recommendations â€” using solved problems, teaching students to recognize algebraic structure, and selecting appropriate solution strategies â€” and uses a Professional Learning Community model with Plan-Do-Study-Act cycles to lower the barrier to implementation. It includes facilitator guides, participant workbooks, data collection tools, and videos for use by individual educators or school teams.

entries.title: REL Northwest's Guide to Community Engagement Data Collection and Analysis
entries.desc: REL Northwest published this 2026 guide to help education agency staff collect, analyze, and act on community engagement input from students, families, educators, and community partners. The guide provides 12 supporting resources organized across three phases â€” before, during, and after engagement â€” including ready-to-use checklists, tip sheets, and templates for research question design, note-taking, codebook generation, data analysis, and findings dissemination. It emphasizes that agencies that demonstrate genuine responsiveness to community feedback build trust and improve program quality.

entries.title: Exploring Implementation of Trauma-Engaged Practices in Alaska Schools
entries.desc: REL Northwest (REL 2025-011) examined how Alaska schools implement trauma-engaged practices using the state's Transforming Schools Framework, drawing on administrative data and surveys. Most schools were at an emerging implementation level; schools with higher awareness of state support resources showed substantially higher implementation. Implementation levels predicted student perceptions on two school climate measures â€” Caring Others and Cultural Connectedness â€” but did not significantly affect other measured outcomes. School leaders identified staff buy-in, professional development, dedicated funding, and community partnerships as critical success factors.

entries.title: Applying a Cycle of Evidence-Based Continuous Improvement When Selecting Interventions and Project Components to Improve Attendance
entries.desc: REL West published this December 2023 fact sheet presenting an overview and worked example of how districts can apply a continuous improvement cycle when selecting attendance interventions or project components to reduce chronic absence. The resource addresses how to identify root causes of absenteeism, select appropriate evidence-based supports, and monitor implementation effectiveness using a structured, data-informed approach. It is designed for district and school leaders working to address chronic absence, which affects approximately 14.8 million students nationwide.

entries.title: Principal Retention Patterns in Arizona, Nevada, and Utah
entries.desc: REL West (REL 2021-129) examined principal retention across Arizona, Nevada, and Utah, finding that fewer than half of principals in each state remained at the same school over the four-year period from fall 2016 to fall 2020. Retention rates varied significantly by school performance level, with substantially lower retention at lower-performing schools. When principals left their positions, those who remained in the profession tended to transfer within the same school district rather than to a different district.

entries.title: Student Group Differences in Arkansas' Indicators of Postsecondary Readiness and Success
entries.desc: REL Southwest (REL 2023-145) analyzed middle and high school indicators predicting postsecondary success in Arkansas using data from grade 6 cohorts from 2008/09â€“2009/10, examining variation by race/ethnicity, economic status, disability status, and other characteristics. Indicator attainment varied substantially across student groups, with the largest differences between students with and without a designated disability; academic indicators showed greater disparities than behavioral ones. Predictive accuracy of indicators remained largely consistent across demographic groups, though specific indicators â€” notably ELA and science proficiency in middle school â€” showed variation in predictive strength for some populations.

entries.title: English Language Development Among American Indian English Learner Students in New Mexico
entries.desc: REL Southwest (REL 2022-135) tracked two cohorts of American Indian English learner students who entered kindergarten in 2013/14 or 2014/15 in New Mexico public schools, examining English proficiency progress over five years. The majority were not reclassified as English proficient within the state's ESSA-aligned five-year goal timeframe, and most did not meet grade-level standards in ELA and math in grades 3 and 4. Students attending a bilingual multicultural education program for at least four years showed higher reclassification and academic achievement rates than peers without such exposure.

entries.title: An Examination of the Costs of Texas Community Colleges
entries.desc: REL Southwest (REL 2023-142) examined whether Texas community colleges have sufficient funding to help all students meet performance-based milestones, using needs analysis, equity analysis, and cost function analysis. Colleges serving higher proportions of first-generation, economically disadvantaged, academically disadvantaged, older, and English learner students earned fewer success point milestones per full-time equivalent student. Current spending levels were found to be insufficient to cover the cost of providing equal opportunity to disadvantaged student populations to achieve outcomes comparable to their peers.

entries.title: Exploring Implementation of Attendance Supports to Reduce Chronic Absenteeism in the Providence Public School District
entries.desc: REL Northeast & Islands (REL 2021-099) examined attendance support initiatives in Providence Public School District during 2018â€“19, identifying which supports were most effective at reducing chronic absenteeism. Schools experiencing decreased chronic absenteeism implemented text messaging, phone calls, and mentorship programs with greater fidelity; multilingual text outreach showed particular promise in reaching families. Schools with declining absenteeism rates adopted attendance-related text messaging faster than schools where absenteeism increased.

entries.title: Ask an Expert: College- and Career-Readiness Interventions
entries.desc: REL Appalachia (published June 2022) compiled this resource identifying 15 college and career readiness interventions that meet ESSA Tier 1, 2, or 3 evidence standards, with particular attention to whether research was conducted in rural areas or with economically disadvantaged students. The resource provides details on each intervention's evidence tier, research context, and outcome domain, helping practitioners in REL Appalachia's region select evidence-based approaches suited to their specific student populations and geographic contexts.

entries.title: High-Quality Early Child Education is Associated With Outcomes
entries.desc: REL West published this November 2025 infographic summarizing research-identified outcomes associated with high-quality early childhood education across four domains: kindergarten readiness, educational progress, long-term outcomes including college enrollment and employment, and health benefits. The summary identifies four essential features of high-quality programs: evidence-based curricula with play-based instruction and teacher coaching; educators with bachelor's degrees and Kâ€“12 equivalent compensation; small class sizes with low staff-to-child ratios; and full-day formats. Disadvantaged populations â€” including low-income children and children of color â€” were found to benefit most substantially.

entries.title: Teacher Certification, Retention, and Recruitment in Palau: Understanding Graduation Patterns of Teacher Education Students at Palau Community College
entries.desc: REL Pacific (REL 2025-008, December 2024) examined how long teacher education students take to complete associate's degree programs at Palau Community College, prompted by Public Law 10-32 requiring all teachers to obtain an associate's degree in education or their subject area by end of 2023. The study aimed to support policymakers in understanding teacher certification timelines and help education leaders in Palau develop evidence-based strategies for training, retaining, and recruiting qualified educators across the Pacific region.

entries.title: Building Pathways for Student Success â€” REL Southwest Arkansas Partnership
entries.desc: REL Southwest partners with the Arkansas Department of Education to enhance college and career readiness through high-quality interventions that strengthen student success pathways. Current work includes coaching to support an inclusive application process and rigorous evaluation design for the THRIVE Initiative, as well as formative evaluation best practices coaching for the Arkansas Department of Education's AIIM Initiative. The partnership applies evidence-based frameworks to help Arkansas identify and scale effective programs targeting underserved student populations.

entries.title: Southwest English Learner Literacy (SWELL) Partnership
entries.desc: REL Southwest collaborates with New Mexico educators through the SWELL partnership to enhance English learner literacy using the Write to Succeed professional learning initiative. The program trains teachers in evidence-based literacy practices emphasizing culturally and linguistically responsive instruction, with peer collaboration and role-play practice before classroom implementation. Resources include teacher peer collaboration tools and professional learning support aligned to the needs of New Mexico's English learner student population.

entries.title: Providing Reading Interventions for Students in Middle School Toolkit
entries.desc: REL Southwest collaborates with the Texas Education Agency and school districts to develop professional development resources and online learning modules for grades 6â€“8 educators aimed at strengthening literacy instruction and improving reading skills. The toolkit includes professional learning communities for peer collaboration, tools to assess teacher practice and implementation fidelity, and resources for institutionalizing ongoing support. The initiative is grounded in the WWC practice guide on providing reading interventions for students in grades 4â€“9.

entries.title: Leading Early Childhood Achievement and Development (LEAD) Partnership
entries.desc: REL Southwest partners with the Oklahoma State Department of Education and local school districts to strengthen educator capacity in research-based early learning practices targeting school leaders and classroom educators. The initiative develops and refines professional learning interventions focused on play-based learning and quality teacher-student interactions to improve academic outcomes. REL Southwest uses an iterative development process to test and refine approaches before broader implementation across Oklahoma.

entries.title: Towards an Evidence Framework for Emerging Educational Technologies
entries.desc: This report summarizes a two-day workshop held in January 2026 that convened 36 educators, technology innovators, researchers, and funders to re-frame how evidence for emerging technologies is generated and used. The workshop aimed to stimulate discussion, imagine new evidence frameworks, and establish a common language across the educational ecosystem. The brief highlights alternative visualizations of evidence, articulates key tensions, and suggests starting points for future work.

entries.title: AI in Education: A Set of Four Essential Resource Briefs for Educators
entries.desc: This set of four resource briefs provides educators with essential knowledge for navigating the emerging AI landscape, designed to support instructional integrity and guide educators and students to become informed, critical, and responsible AI users. The briefs address four pillars: AI Literacy Basics, Creating Responsible AI Use Guidance, Effective Classroom Practices with AI, and Misconceptions About AI. Together they bridge the gap between AI technology and responsible instructional practice.

entries.title: Operationalizing AI in K-12 Education: Five District Use Cases
entries.desc: Developed through the U-GAIN Reading R&D Center, this report translates five critical EdTech recommendations into practical district use cases for building capacity, ensuring governance, and maintaining instructional integrity. Drawing on examples from three districts—Orcutt Union, Indian Prairie, and Allentown—the report illustrates how to build collaborative networks, develop cross-functional procurement teams, tailor professional learning, set guardrails, and establish AI-specific accountability systems. It offers school leaders concrete models for responsible and effective AI adoption.

entries.title: Research and Development in America's Schools
entries.desc: This report examines how school systems build and implement research and development (R&D) to drive sustainable improvement and innovation, drawing on direct work with schools and a national landscape analysis. It includes tools for school systems to assess their R&D practices and provides recommendations for deepening the use of R&D to accelerate innovation. The report highlights the conditions, practices, and real-world examples that enable system-level R&D grounded in evidence and local context.

entries.title: How R&D Infrastructure for Postsecondary Education Could Unlock New Advances
entries.desc: This paper articulates a vision for a modern postsecondary R&D infrastructure to overcome systemic barriers—including faculty autonomy, non-standardized assessments, and siloed student data—that impede large-scale instructional innovation. Examining initiatives within the SEERNet research network, including Arizona State University's Learning@Scale data warehouse and the Terracotta and OpenStax Kinetic experimental tools, the author demonstrates how integrated datasets and open-access instruments can lower barriers to rigorous experimentation. The paper calls for an ethos of openness and public investment in research infrastructure to prevent privatization of educational insights.

entries.title: Privacy Enhancing Technologies in Digital Learning Platforms
entries.desc: This report argues that Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs)—including encryption, differential privacy, and synthetic data—should be treated as core research infrastructure for digital learning platforms, functioning as both technical tools and social technologies for responsible data governance. PETs allow valuable analyses such as securely linking education and workforce records or comparing outcomes across sites, while meeting the ethical obligation to protect students, many of whom are minors. The paper is part of the SEERNet initiative's work on building trustworthy research infrastructure for educational platforms.

entries.title: What States Say about Evaluating AI in Education
entries.desc: This report examines state-level AI in education evaluation guidance across 32 U.S. states and Puerto Rico, categorizing state AI guidance across three developmental stages: Nascent & Exploratory, Emergent & Piloting, and Systematic & Evidentiary. Findings show a majority of states engaged in exploratory activities with few conducting systematic, large-scale assessment of AI-enabled edtech's impact on student learning outcomes. The report provides recommendations for progressing through evaluation stages and integrating co-design feedback loop processes into evaluation activities.

entries.title: Promoting Mathematical Reasoning in OpenSciEd Middle School Curriculum Units
entries.desc: This study responds to the need to support science teachers in deepening students' engagement in mathematical reasoning, collaborating with two districts to explore integration of mathematics into middle school science classrooms using OpenSciEd instructional materials. The research team designed professional learning experiences and resources to help teachers deepen their mathematics knowledge, support students' mathematical reasoning, and modify instructional materials to enhance connections to mathematics. Findings indicate that teachers can enhance student engagement in mathematics during phenomenon-based science instruction given appropriate resources and support.

entries.title: AI Literacy in PK-12 Education
entries.desc: This brief (part of a set sharing AI literacy recommendations for various educational audiences) provides guidance to make progress in PK-12 AI literacy implementation, which has gained widespread support at national, state, and local levels with momentum building across computer science courses and interdisciplinary integrations. It recommends a flexible, multi-level approach that reflects the need for AI literacy guidance to be adaptable across learning contexts rather than prescribing specific tools or methods. The brief is designed to support educational leaders and policymakers in developing AI literacy policies and pathways.

entries.title: AI Literacy in Postsecondary Education
entries.desc: This brief (part of a set sharing AI literacy recommendations for various educational audiences) outlines foundational AI skills and competencies for postsecondary educators and learners alongside key gaps in AI literacy for these groups. It suggests recommendations for reducing AI literacy gaps in the postsecondary setting, including establishing comprehensive institutional AI governance frameworks while implementing and sustaining context-specific educator development and support programs. The brief is focused on providing actionable guidance to postsecondary educational leaders and policymakers.

entries.title: Integrating AI-Enabled EdTech in PK16 Education: Five Recommendations
entries.desc: This brief provides five actionable recommendations for state, district, and local leaders to guide the responsible integration of AI-enabled educational technologies across PK-16 systems, warning that without careful planning, governance, and investment, AI tools risk widening equity gaps and undermining trust. The recommendations focus on collaborative adoption, transparent governance, professional learning, classroom integration, and accountability systems. The brief aims to ensure AI-enabled edtech strengthens teaching and learning while safeguarding learners and supporting equitable access to future-ready opportunities.

entries.title: AI-Enabled Edtech Adoption Cycle
entries.desc: The AI-enabled edtech adoption cycle presents four interconnected phases—Vision and Planning, Implementation, Ongoing Evaluation, and Vision and Planning—emphasizing that AI-enabled EdTech adoption is not a linear process but an ongoing cycle of improvement. The framework requires intentional planning, collaboration, and commitment to data-driven refinement throughout each phase. By integrating these considerations, institutions can more effectively leverage AI technologies to enhance teaching and learning outcomes while ensuring sustainable, equitable implementation.

entries.title: A Framework for Powerful Learning with Emerging Technology
entries.desc: This framework puts forward research-based, field-informed guidelines for designing and implementing emerging technology tools in ways that are evidence-based, learner-centered, and skill-building, developed through interviews with more than 50 experts, collaboration with a working group of 10 distinguished professionals, and a literature review. It considers both the opportunities and challenges that come with emerging technologies, offering guidance to education leaders, educators, learners, and edtech developers. The framework identifies key principles, practices, and strategies to support powerful learning through emerging technology.

entries.title: Researching Digital Learning Platforms: Foundations, Methods, and Policy Recommendations
entries.desc: This working paper observes that educational research on technology has been accelerating rapidly but that platform data is most often used only minimally as a measure of usage or dosage, while a smaller subfield dives more deeply into digital learning platform (DLP) data streams. It gives examples of three types of theories connected to DLP data, illustrates the range of rigorous methods that have been successfully employed, and addresses emerging work on using DLP data to study responsible AI use. The authors recommend bringing attention to the DLP-as-research-infrastructure movement as a distinct and growing subfield that can produce actionable insights for product developers, education leaders, and educators.

entries.title: A National Context of Generative AI for Reading to Support English Learners
entries.desc: This U-GAIN Reading R&D Center report investigates generative AI applications for enhancing reading instruction to achieve equitable gains for diverse elementary students, including English Learners, combining an edtech market scan with interviews and listening sessions involving 16 educators across 11 states. Findings indicate that districts prioritize the Science of Reading and that edtech platforms show a trend toward integrating social-emotional learning, personalized tutoring, and real-time assessments. Key opportunities include customizing texts to student interests and reading levels and facilitating adaptive activities, but concerns emerged about AI biases, Automated Speech Recognition inaccuracies for diverse speech patterns, and student data privacy.

entries.title: Glossary of Artificial Intelligence Terms for Educators
entries.desc: This bilingual (English and Spanish) resource serves as an essential reference guide for educators learning about and implementing AI systems and tools, providing clear, accessible definitions of key AI concepts including algorithms, machine learning, and neural networks. The glossary maintains a focus on learning applications and addresses critical considerations such as bias in training data, interpretable machine learning, and human-centered approaches to AI implementation. It was authored with collaboration from Fundación Universitaria del Área Andina and emphasizes the importance of maintaining human agency in learning settings, advocating for AI as a tool to augment rather than replace teachers.

entries.title: Unlocking the Potential of Technology Innovations: A Vision for Supporting Multilingual Learners
entries.desc: This report presents a landscape study examining how technology and digital media are currently leveraged to support young multilingual learners (MLLs) ages 3–8, combining a comprehensive research literature review, an exploratory resource landscape scan, and interviews with leading educational technology and digital media designers and researchers. The study recognizes multilingualism as an asset and documents a need and opportunity for research and development efforts to center MLLs and ensure digital tools are designed to effectively support their learning. Findings inform design and research recommendations for future efforts aimed at creating edtech with and for MLLs.

entries.title: From Here to There (FH2T): Educator Perceptions of Game-Based Algebraic Learning
entries.desc: Building on prior efficacy research, Digital Promise facilitated a Research-Practice-Industry Partnership (RPIP) and confirmatory study to explore the usability, feasibility, and scalability of the FH2T game-based algebra tool, bringing together algebra educators, product developers from Graspable Math, and learning scientists. The research goal was to understand how FH2T could be implemented at scale and how digital tools can be used to democratize complex mathematical concepts for students. The project employed professional learning, co-design, and research activities to ensure each group's voice was heard and power dynamics were ameliorated.

entries.title: Portraits in Progress: Emerging Future-Ready Skills Assessment Strategies
entries.desc: As schools, districts, and states establish Portraits of a Graduate, this report details assessment strategies being used to assess future-ready skills—including performance-based assessments, defenses of learning, and badging—finding that districts prioritize student-centered assessments emphasizing growth, self-evaluation, and feedback. The authors recommend that districts clarify their purpose for future-ready skills assessment and align their assessment system with their instructional vision. Policy recommendations include investing in research to create reliable and valid tools for assessing future-ready skills and aligning recognition systems across K–12, postsecondary education, and the workforce.

entries.title: Expected and Emerging Requirements for Digital Learning Platforms as Research Infrastructure
entries.desc: This white paper examines progress made by digital learning platforms (DLPs) in the SEERNet initiative—funded by the Institute of Education Sciences—which aims to advance research infrastructure by bridging the gap between practice and research. Expected requirements addressed include enhancing platform data capabilities, enabling experimental research designs, and protecting data privacy, while emerging needs included standardized workflows, researcher outreach strategies, and templates for IRB approvals and data-sharing agreements. The paper reflects on lessons learned from SEERNet's efforts, highlighting innovations in data documentation, experimental workflows, and collaborative relationship building.

entries.title: Connecting SEERNet and Improvement Science to Pursue Better Outcomes in Schools
entries.desc: This white paper explores how SEERNet—a network of digital learning platforms, researchers, and educators—can be integrated with Improvement Science methodologies to rapidly test and scale solutions for student learning challenges. It examines the collaborative power of Networked Improvement Communities (NICs) and illustrates through a scenario how a school district could use iterative Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) cycles to address reading comprehension barriers in STEM subjects for English learners and students with disabilities. The paper concludes with five recommendations: fostering collaboration, enhancing data sharing, leveraging root cause analysis, implementing iterative improvements, and scaling successful interventions.

entries.title: Practitioner-reported Needs for Enacting, Implementing, and Adopting OpenSciEd Curriculum Materials
entries.desc: This report identifies OpenSciEd practitioners' most urgent needs through survey responses from 155 teachers and leaders and focus group interviews with 28 respondents, finding three broad challenge areas related to district adoption and implementation, classroom enactment and engagement, and obtaining evidence of NGSS-based student outcomes. Seven themes emerged for supporting practitioners through research, development, and innovation, with professional learning and other supports for teachers appearing to address root causes of many challenges. The report provides direction to researchers, practitioners, policymakers, and developers on how OpenSciEd-enabled research could drive system-level change in science education.

entries.title: Social Regulation of Learning
entries.desc: This primer explores social regulation of learning as a critical component of successful collaborative learning, encompassing self-regulation, co-regulation, and socially shared regulation of learning, and discusses how these components help students navigate social, cognitive, and emotional challenges within collaborative settings. The primer highlights the teacher's role in fostering regulation skills, the importance of a positive classroom climate, and the need for students to be given time in groups without their teacher to develop shared regulation skills. It provides practical indicators and strategies for educators to enhance collaborative learning experiences, integrating newer research through the eyes of practitioners and researchers working toward deeper understanding and application.

entries.title: Collaborative Argumentation for Learning
entries.desc: This primer explores the benefits and challenges of integrating collaborative argumentation practices into classrooms to deepen student understanding and foster critical thinking, identifying argumentation as a key approach for building scientific knowledge and improving content learning. The document outlines strategies for supporting students' argumentation skills, fostering a culture of critical communication, and overcoming obstacles such as time constraints and lack of systemic support. Recommendations include introducing argumentation as knowledge building and consensus, establishing a classroom culture of respect and curiosity, incorporating multiple perspectives, and creating authentic opportunities for in-depth argumentation.

entries.title: Assessment During Collaborative Learning
entries.desc: This primer explores assessment during collaborative learning, shifting focus from traditional summative evaluation to ongoing formative assessment, and addresses key questions about what should be assessed—individual knowledge, group dynamics, collaborative skills, or some combination. The document outlines effective practices for incorporating self- and peer-assessment to empower students, provides guidance on delivering meaningful feedback, and discusses balancing individual and group assessments to ensure students learn content and collaborate successfully. It offers practical strategies, example assessments, and research-based insights to facilitate application of these principles in classroom settings.

entries.title: Classroom Discourse
entries.desc: This primer explores student-centered classroom discourse, examining its benefits and challenges and providing strategies for effective implementation, emphasizing a shift from traditional "Initiation-Response-Evaluation" patterns to interactive approaches that enhance engagement, deeper processing, reasoning skills, and active listening. The document discusses methods such as "accountable talk" and "exploratory talk," highlights the crucial role of teachers in modeling effective talk moves and setting clear expectations, and addresses challenges such as unequal participation and power dynamics. Practical strategies include establishing ground rules, planning thought-provoking questions, using diverse talk formats, and incorporating talk moves to foster productive classroom discussions.

entries.title: Voices of Undergraduate Students Taking Introductory Statistics Courses
entries.desc: This report presents findings from a 2023–24 study by Achieving the Dream examining the experiences of 50 undergraduate students in introductory statistics courses using Lumen One courseware, highlighting the effectiveness of courseware features providing formative practice with feedback. The study explores how instructor involvement and course modality influence student engagement and sense of belonging, particularly among students of color, finding that about half of students indicated their instructors assisted them in connecting courseware content to their understanding. Recommendations emphasize improving feedback mechanisms and increasing instructor engagement to better support student success, while also noting that students appreciated real-world examples and low-stakes assessments.

entries.title: NAEP — The Nation's Report Card
entries.desc: The Nation's Report Card (NAEP) is the largest nationally representative and continuing assessment of what America's students know and can do across subjects including mathematics, reading, science, civics, and others. It provides a common measure of student achievement that offers a window into the state of the K-12 education system, with results reported at national, state, and district levels. Data tools allow users to explore NAEP results, access assessment items, and download datasets for research.

entries.title: IPEDS — Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System
entries.desc: IPEDS is the primary source for data on U.S. colleges, universities, and technical and vocational institutions, consisting of 12 interrelated survey components conducted annually that gather data from every institution participating in federal student financial aid programs. Data collections cover enrollment, completions, graduation rates, finance, human resources, student financial aid, and more, with releases each fall, winter, and spring. Users can access institutional data through interactive tools, create custom analyses, or download full survey datasets for research.

entries.title: Common Core of Data (CCD)
entries.desc: The Common Core of Data (CCD) is the Department of Education's primary database on public elementary and secondary education in the United States, comprising a comprehensive annual national database of all public elementary and secondary schools and school districts. CCD collects nonfiscal data through the EDFacts office and fiscal data separately, with publications covering school directories, dropout and graduation rates, district finances, and enrollment. Researchers can search the bibliography for articles, books, and dissertations based on CCD data, and use the EDFIN tool for public school finance information.

entries.title: Early Childhood Longitudinal Study (ECLS)
entries.desc: The Early Childhood Longitudinal Study (ECLS) program provides information about children's knowledge, skills, and development from birth through elementary school, comprising four longitudinal studies that examine child development, school readiness, and early school experiences. Studies include a birth cohort (ECLS-B) following children born in 2001, the kindergarten classes of 1998–99 and 2010–11, and the newest ECLS-K:2024 following the kindergarten class of 2023–24 through third grade. The program provides data to analyze relationships among family, school, community, and individual factors with children's development and early learning.

entries.title: High School Longitudinal Study of 2009 (HSLS:09)
entries.desc: HSLS:09 is a nationally representative longitudinal study of more than 23,000 9th graders from 944 schools in 2009, with follow-ups in 2012 and 2016 and postsecondary administrative records through 2021. The study includes surveys of students, parents, math and science teachers, school administrators, and counselors, along with a student assessment in algebraic skills, reasoning, and problem solving. Data collection waves span from the 2009 base year through postsecondary transcripts, enabling research on transitions from high school into college and the workforce, including 10 state-representative datasets.

entries.title: Education Longitudinal Study of 2002 (ELS:2002)
entries.desc: ELS:2002 is a nationally representative longitudinal study of 10th graders in 2002, following students through secondary and postsecondary years with data collection waves spanning from the 2002 base year through a 2012 third follow-up and 2013 postsecondary transcripts. The study focuses on students' trajectories from high school into postsecondary education, the workforce, and beyond, including patterns of college access and persistence, with surveys of students, parents, math and English teachers, and school administrators. High school transcripts and postsecondary transcripts are available for research on coursetaking and grades; public-use data are accessible through the NCES DataLab.

entries.title: National Education Longitudinal Study of 1988 (NELS:88)
entries.desc: NELS:88 is a nationally representative longitudinal study of 8th graders in 1988, following students through secondary and postsecondary years across four follow-ups through 2000, 12 years after the baseline survey. The study collected surveys from students on school, work, and home experiences, educational aspirations, and peer and parent influences, along with student assessments in reading, social studies, mathematics, and science at 8th, 10th, and 12th grades. High school and postsecondary transcript data are available, and the study was designed to provide trend data on critical transitions from middle school through postsecondary education and the labor force.

entries.title: Beginning Postsecondary Students Longitudinal Study (BPS)
entries.desc: The Beginning Postsecondary Students (BPS) Longitudinal Study surveys cohorts of first-time beginning students at three points: at the end of their first year, and three and six years after first starting postsecondary education. Cohorts are drawn from the National Postsecondary Student Aid Study (NPSAS), with the most recent completed study (BPS:12/17) containing data on approximately 22,500 students and the current cohort (BPS:20/22) representing approximately 3.3 million undergraduates who first enrolled in 2019–20. Data track students' paths through postsecondary education and address policy questions about completion, financial aid, and credential attainment.

entries.title: Civil Rights Data Collection (CRDC)
entries.source: U.S. Department of Education Office for Civil Rights
entries.desc: The Civil Rights Data Collection (CRDC) has collected civil rights data related to students' access and barriers to educational opportunity from early childhood through grade 12 since 1968, and since the 2011–12 collection has been administered every two years to all public school districts and schools in the 50 states and Washington, D.C. The CRDC collects information on student enrollment, access to courses, programs, and school staff, and school climate factors such as bullying, harassment, and student discipline, with most data disaggregated by race, ethnicity, sex, disability, and English Learner status. It is a key enforcement tool under civil rights laws prohibiting discrimination based on race, color, national origin, sex, disability, and age.

entries.title: Urban Institute Education Data Explorer
entries.desc: The Urban Institute's Education Data Explorer aggregates data from multiple federal and other education sources into a single platform, allowing users to build custom datasets by narrowing by education level, geography, timeframe, and indicator. The tool is designed to make it easier to generate rigorous, accurate, and actionable insights to improve student outcomes, with access available through an interactive explorer or a full API for programmatic use. The platform is oriented toward evidence-based policy decisions and is freely accessible under published terms of use.

entries.title: National Student Clearinghouse Research Center
entries.desc: The National Student Clearinghouse Research Center publishes relevant, objective data on postsecondary metrics using enrollment and completion records from the majority of U.S. institutions, with publication series covering undergraduate degree earners, transfer outcomes, enrollment trends, persistence and retention, and high school benchmark data. Reports track cohort outcomes longitudinally—for example, reporting that the national 6-year college completion rate for fall 2019 starters was 61.1%—and provide comparisons by student demographics, institution type, and state. Data and reports guide policymakers and institutional leaders on issues of college access, transfer, and completion.

entries.title: PISA — Programme for International Student Assessment (Data)
entries.desc: The PISA database contains the full set of responses from individual students, school principals, and parents, available for download in SPSS and SAS formats across assessment cycles from 2000 to 2022. Designed for statisticians and professional researchers, the files include background questionnaires, codebooks, and compendia alongside analytical tools such as the PISA Education GPS for downloading full indicator sets and generating country profile reports. The OECD also provides the PISA Data Analysis Manual and IEA IDB Analyzer software to support accurate use of the complex survey methodology.

entries.title: TIMSS — Trends in International Mathematics and Science Study (Databases)
entries.desc: The TIMSS and PIRLS International Databases are publicly available to researchers, analysts, and anyone interested in improving education, with the aim of supporting broad use of the data. The databases include student mathematics, science, and reading achievement data as well as student, parent, teacher, school, and curricular background data for all participating countries across multiple assessment cycles. A public use version of the datasets is available for download, covering TIMSS cycles from 1995 through 2023 and TIMSS Advanced assessments.

entries.title: PIRLS 2021 — Progress in International Reading Literacy Study (International Database)
entries.desc: The PIRLS 2021 International Database includes student reading achievement data and student, home, teacher, school, and national context data for 57 countries and 8 benchmarking participants, representing the fifth PIRLS cycle since 2001 and 20 years of trend data at the fourth grade. Because data collection occurred during COVID-19 school disruptions, the database provides a uniquely rich source for researching pandemic impacts on teaching and learning reading. Data are available in R, SPSS, and SAS formats along with codebooks, item parameters, almanacs, and analysis programs; a restricted-use version is also available through the IEA Study Data Repository for variables removed from the public release.

entries.title: World Bank EdStats — Education Statistics Portal
entries.desc: The World Bank EdStats Portal provides easy access to key education indicators spanning access, completion, learning outcomes, expenditures, policy, and equity from pre-primary through tertiary levels.
