In addition to the core EdStats database, the portal links to specialized datasets including the Learning Poverty database, the Harmonized Learning Outcomes database, and Country Learning Outcomes (CLO) of harmonized learning assessments. The portal is currently being revamped to improve user experience and relevance, with all datasets remaining accessible via direct download links during the transition.

entries.title: ICPSR — Education Research Studies
entries.desc: ICPSR's searchable archive contains over 11,000 published education-related studies, spanning topics from early childhood and K-12 schooling to higher education finance, adult education, and special education. Studies are contributed by investigators across government agencies and universities and are available with associated variables (87,000+), series (309), and data-related publications (7,800+). The archive supports filtering by subject terms, geography, restriction type, data format, collection method, and funding agency, enabling targeted access to replication data and primary survey datasets.

entries.title: Opportunity Insights — Education and Mobility Data Library
entries.desc: Opportunity Insights publishes a publicly available data library of datasets produced alongside its research studies, enabling other researchers and practitioners to analyze social mobility, education, life expectancy, patent rates, and other outcomes by neighborhood, college, parental income level, and racial background. Datasets are provided in CSV and Stata formats with codebooks and replication code, and cover topics including college-level data for 139 selective American colleges, social capital by county, student loan balances, and cohort-level trends in outcomes from 1978 to 1992 birth cohorts. Users can filter the library by geographic level, topic, and paper title, and contact the team for technical assistance.

entries.title: Stanford Education Data Archive (SEDA)
entries.desc: The Stanford Education Data Archive, produced by the Educational Opportunity Project at Stanford University, is the first national database of academic performance measuring educational opportunity in every community in America across reading and math. The archive supports three active projects — the 2009-2019 Educational Opportunity Project (updated February 2026 with new trend data), the Education Recovery Project tracking learning loss and pandemic recovery across districts, and the Segregation Tracking Project mapping school and neighborhood segregation over decades. Data, interactive explorers, and research papers are freely available, with the most recent update adding new opportunity, recovery, and segregation data.

entries.title: PSLC DataShop KDD Cup 2010 Educational Data Mining Challenge
entries.desc: The KDD Cup 2010 challenge asked participants to predict student performance on mathematical problems from logs of student interaction with Intelligent Tutoring Systems, using five datasets (three development, two challenge) drawn from real ITS interactions. The task explored how generally or narrowly students learn, how quickly they improve, and whether knowledge requirements of problems can be inferred directly from student performance data. Post-competition submissions and a leaderboard remain available for continued benchmarking.

entries.title: LearnSphere — Education Research Data Infrastructure
entries.desc: LearnSphere integrates existing and new educational data and analysis repositories to offer what it describes as the world's largest learning analytics infrastructure, combining linked data, analysis methods, and portal access to resources including DataShop, MOOCdb, DiscourseDB, and DataStage. The platform's Tigris workflow authoring tool facilitates the creation and sharing of custom analyses across multiple institutions including CMU, Memphis, Stanford, and ALTTAI. LearnSphere supports data-driven course design and large-scale distributed data infrastructure for education researchers.

entries.title: Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD)
entries.desc: The Open University Learning Analytics Dataset (OULAD) contains anonymised data about courses, students, and their interactions with the Virtual Learning Environment (VLE) for seven selected Open University modules, with course presentations marked by start month. The dataset consists of tables stored in CSV format connected via unique identifiers, covering student demographic data and fine-grained VLE activity logs. OULAD has been used in learning analytics hackathons at multiple international conferences and received the Educational Dataset Prize from the Educational Data Mining Society in 2023.

entries.title: ASSISTments Dataset
entries.desc: The ASSISTments data repository hosts multiple publicly released datasets from the ASSISTments intelligent tutoring platform, covering student interactions, skill builder sequences, affect data, and log files from various research studies published from 2009 onward. All datasets are scrubbed of personally identifiable information and released under IRB-approved terms of use; researchers who discover residual PII are required to report and delete it. The project reflects a commitment to open science, requiring graduate students to release anonymized data and code alongside each published paper.

entries.title: EdNet — Large-Scale Hierarchical Dataset in Education
entries.desc: EdNet is a large-scale dataset of student-system interactions collected over two years by Santa, a multi-platform AI tutoring service with more than 780,000 users in Korea, comprising 131 million interactions from 784,309 students across iOS, Android, and web platforms. The dataset records diverse student actions — including which learning materials were consumed, responses, time spent, and lecture-watching — organized into a four-level hierarchy (KT1 through KT4) of increasing interaction richness. EdNet is described as the largest publicly available education dataset in terms of total students, interactions, and interaction types, and is accompanied by a published paper (arXiv:1912.03072) and an open leaderboard.

entries.title: Duolingo Shared Task on Second Language Acquisition Modeling (SLAM)
entries.desc: The 2018 Duolingo Shared Task on Second Language Acquisition Modeling (SLAM) provided participating teams with transcripts from millions of exercises completed by thousands of Duolingo learners over their first 30 days of study, annotated at the token level for mistakes, with the task of predicting future learner errors. Three tracks covered learners of English, Spanish, and French, enabling research into personalized adaptive SLA modeling and language forgetting over time; 15 teams participated and the best systems improved upon a logistic regression baseline by 12%. The task was conducted in conjunction with the 13th BEA Workshop and NAACL-HLT 2018.

entries.title: NBER Education Research Data
entries.desc: The NBER Public Use Data Archive is a repository of public-use economic, demographic, and enterprise datasets assembled to support NBER-affiliated researchers, including datasets relevant to education such as school district data, birth and natality records, and population survey files. Files are often reformatted for research convenience relative to original sources, and the archive also hosts data and code outputs from NBER research projects intended for replication. Researchers can request that the NBER host data or code from NBER-associated papers, projects, or grants via a formal submission process.

entries.title: TALIS — Teaching and Learning International Survey
entries.desc: TALIS is the world's largest international survey about teachers and school leaders. Since 2008, it has gathered information from teachers and school leaders about working conditions and learning environments in over 60 education systems. TALIS 2024 sampled approximately 280,000 teachers in 17,000 schools across 55 education systems. The survey covers ISCED 2 teachers and school leaders, with additional modules for ISCED 1 and ISCED 3, as well as a Teacher Knowledge Survey (TKS) module. A companion survey, TALIS Starting Strong, focuses on early childhood staff and centre leaders. Results are used by policymakers to improve teaching and learning around the world; the next cycle is planned for 2030.

entries.title: PIAAC — Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies
entries.desc: PIAAC is a programme of assessment and analysis of adult skills whose major product is the Survey of Adult Skills — an international computer-based household survey of adults aged 16–65, designed as 10-yearly cycles. The survey measures adults' proficiency in literacy, numeracy, and problem solving, and collects data on how adults use these skills at home and at work. The 1st Cycle was conducted in three rounds between 2011 and 2018 across 39 countries, interviewing approximately 245,000 adults representing 1.15 billion people. A 2nd Cycle covering 31 countries and economies completed its first round of data collection in 2022–2023, with results released in December 2024. Data support evidence-based decisions in workforce development, lifelong learning, and social inclusion.

entries.title: National Teacher and Principal Survey (NTPS)
entries.desc: The National Teacher and Principal Survey (NTPS) is a system of related questionnaires that provides descriptive data on the context of public and private elementary and secondary education in the United States. Redesigned from the Schools and Staffing Survey (SASS), NTPS collects information directly from teachers and principals to provide local, state, and national policymakers with statistics on the condition of education. The survey supports analysis of principal, teacher, and student characteristics in detail and enables subgroup analysis (e.g., by gender, race/ethnicity, school locale). Paired with Teacher Follow-up Surveys (TFS) and Principal Follow-up Surveys (PFS), NTPS tracks teacher and principal attrition and mobility. The most recent main collection covered 2020–21, with follow-up surveys in 2021–22.

entries.title: National Household Education Surveys Program (NHES)
entries.desc: The National Household Education Surveys Program (NHES) is the flagship household survey program of the National Center for Education Statistics (NCES). It collects nationally representative, descriptive data on the educational activities of children and families in the United States. Surveys are conducted every three to four years on rotating topics; current focus areas include early childhood care and education, parent and family involvement in schools, and homeschooling. Data enable trend analysis over time. The most recent administration concluded in August 2023, with reports released in September 2024 covering early childhood program participation and parent and family involvement in education.

entries.title: EDFacts Data Files
entries.desc: EDFacts is a U.S. Department of Education initiative to collect, analyze, and promote the use of high-quality pre-kindergarten through grade 12 data. It centralizes data provided by state education agencies (SEAs), including district and school demographics, program participation, and performance data. EDFacts integrates SEA-supplied performance data with other departmental data assets (such as financial grant information) to support planning, policymaking, and budget decision-making at federal, state, and local levels. The initiative aims to place robust, timely performance data at the core of education decision-making, reduce state and district data reporting burden, and improve state data capabilities through technical assistance.

entries.title: National Assessment of Adult Literacy (NAAL)
entries.desc: The National Assessment of Adult Literacy (NAAL) is a comprehensive national assessment measuring English language literacy skills among American adults aged 16 and older, representing the nation's most comprehensive measure of adult literacy since the 1992 National Adult Literacy Survey. The 2003 survey involved over 19,000 participants across all 50 states and D.C., plus approximately 1,200 incarcerated individuals in federal and state prisons. The assessment covers reading proficiency, health literacy, and background characteristics, providing demographic breakdowns that connect socioeconomic factors to literacy outcomes for researchers, policymakers, and practitioners.

entries.title: Beginning Teacher Longitudinal Study (BTLS)
entries.desc: The Beginning Teacher Longitudinal Study (BTLS) followed a nationally representative cohort of beginning public school teachers first interviewed during the 2007–08 Schools and Staffing Survey through the 2011–12 school year. Unlike typical teacher surveys that provide only a momentary snapshot, BTLS tracked the same cohort over five years to examine career development, attrition, and retention patterns. The study collected data on base salaries, certificate renewals, content area additions, and participation in leadership activities, providing a longitudinal view of early-career teacher trajectories.

entries.title: Career and Technical Education Statistics (CTE Stats)
entries.desc: The Career and Technical Education (CTE) Statistics program delivers comprehensive national data on CTE participation and outcomes from high school through adult and postsecondary education, examining student enrollment patterns, teacher workforce characteristics, and coursetaking by student demographics. The program tracks CTE concentrators across secondary, postsecondary subbaccalaureate, and adult credential programs, and compares their educational trajectories against non-concentrators. Findings from the 2013 high school graduate cohort show CTE concentrators were more likely to earn associate's degrees but less likely to earn bachelor's degrees compared to peers.

entries.title: Middle Grades Longitudinal Study of 2017–18 (MGLS:2017)
entries.desc: The Middle Grades Longitudinal Study of 2017–18 (MGLS:2017) tracked a nationally representative sample of approximately 13,300 sixth-graders across 570 schools to examine the academic, social-emotional, and environmental factors shaping student success and high school readiness during the middle grades transition. Data were collected from five respondent types—students, parents, teachers, special education staff, and school administrators—covering mathematics and reading assessments, executive function, family involvement, and school context. Two collection waves (spring 2018 and spring 2020) followed students through eighth grade, with the second wave shifting to remote participation due to the COVID-19 pandemic; restricted-use files are available to licensed researchers.

entries.title: School Pulse Panel (SPP)
entries.desc: The School Pulse Panel (SPP) surveys a nationally representative sample of public K-12 schools on high-priority education topics once a month during the school year, providing near-real-time data on school operations and student outcomes. The program collects information on student attendance rates, academic readiness, tutoring availability, and other operational metrics, with interactive results organized by topic, geography, and school characteristics. Recent findings (June 2025) indicate that 31% of students were reported as behind grade level, 85% of schools offered tutoring services, and the average daily attendance rate was 91%.

entries.title: National Postsecondary Student Aid Study (NPSAS)
entries.desc: The National Postsecondary Student Aid Study (NPSAS) examines the characteristics of students enrolled in postsecondary education with a special focus on how they finance their education, integrating student surveys, institutional records, and government administrative data. Conducted every three to four years since 1987, the study covers nationally representative samples of undergraduate and graduate students across all 50 states, D.C., and Puerto Rico. Recent 2019–20 findings show that 72% of undergraduates received financial aid averaging $14,100, while 74% of graduate students received aid averaging $25,300; the study also serves as the sampling frame for NCES longitudinal follow-up studies.

entries.title: Baccalaureate and Beyond Longitudinal Study (B&B)
entries.desc: The Baccalaureate and Beyond Longitudinal Study (B&B) examines education and work experiences after bachelor's degree completion, with particular emphasis on new elementary and secondary teachers, tracking cohorts at one, four, and ten years post-graduation. Four cohorts have been followed, with the most recent completing degrees in 2015–16; the study collects data on workforce participation, earnings, graduate school enrollment, student loan debt, homeownership, and teaching careers. Recent findings show that among 2015–16 graduates, 41% pursued additional education within four years, 74% worked full-time, and significant gender-based salary gaps existed one year after graduation.

entries.title: Schools and Staffing Survey (SASS)
entries.desc: The Schools and Staffing Survey (SASS) was an integrated national study of public and private school districts, schools, principals, and teachers designed to provide descriptive data on the context of elementary and secondary education. Conducted seven times between 1987 and 2011, SASS gathered data on teacher demand and compensation, principal and teacher characteristics, school climate, hiring and retention practices, and student demographics. The program was supplemented by the Teacher Follow-Up Survey and Principal Follow-Up Survey tracking mobility and attrition, and was subsequently redesigned and relaunched as the National Teacher and Principal Survey in 2015–16.

entries.title: Private School Universe Survey (PSS)
entries.desc: The Private School Universe Survey (PSS) generates biennial data on private schools, teachers, and students in the United States, targeting all institutions not primarily supported by public funds that provide classroom instruction for grades K–12 and employ at least one teacher. Collecting data since 1989–90, the survey gathers information on religious orientation, school level and size, enrollment, high school graduates, gender composition, teacher counts, program emphasis, and kindergarten offerings. The PSS produces data comparable to the Common Core of Data for public schools, enabling cross-sector analysis of enrollment trends including growth patterns in religiously-affiliated schools.

entries.title: School Survey on Crime and Safety (SSOCS)
entries.desc: The School Survey on Crime and Safety (SSOCS) is the primary source of school-level data on crime and safety for the U.S. Department of Education, collecting information from approximately 4,800 nationally representative public elementary and secondary schools. The survey covers topics related to school crime, violence, discipline, and safety conditions, with periodic data collection and public-use data files available through NCES DataLab. Recent data releases include 2021–22 school year findings alongside historical data through 2019–20, making SSOCS an essential resource for understanding trends in school safety conditions over time.

entries.title: High School and Beyond Longitudinal Study (HS&B)
entries.desc: The High School and Beyond Longitudinal Study (HS&B) is the sixth in a series of NCES longitudinal studies, originally tracking nationally representative cohorts of 10th and 12th graders from 1980 through multiple follow-up surveys to examine how educational system factors influence students' educational and career outcomes. The study collected student and parent questionnaires, cognitive assessments, and high school and postsecondary transcripts, capturing trajectories from high school into the workforce and postsecondary education. Notable findings include associations between advanced high school coursetaking (Algebra II or higher) and lower unemployment at midlife, and data on young adult living arrangements across cohorts.

entries.title: National Longitudinal Study of the High School Class of 1972 (NLS-72)
entries.desc: The National Longitudinal Study of the High School Class of 1972 (NLS-72) pioneered longitudinal research methodology for examining how high school experiences predict career and educational outcomes, following over 21,000 high school seniors through periodic surveys until 1986, with postsecondary transcripts collected in 1984. The study intentionally oversampled schools serving disadvantaged and minority populations and linked educational experiences with early labor market participation and postsecondary enrollment. Findings include that over 90% of participants engaged in paid work within two years of graduation, and the study established a foundational design replicated in subsequent NCES longitudinal programs.

entries.title: High School Transcript Studies (HST)
entries.desc: The High School Transcript Studies (HST) collect official academic records from high school students to examine coursetaking patterns and predict future educational outcomes, gathering data on courses taken, credits earned, course sequencing, final grades, class rank, and standardized test scores where available. Transcript data were collected in conjunction with multiple NCES longitudinal studies (HS&B, NELS:88, ELS:2002, HSLS:09) and the National Assessment of Educational Progress (NAEP), spanning collection years from 1982 to 2019. All data are classified using the standardized six-digit Classification of Secondary School Courses coding system and released only as restricted-use files to licensed researchers to protect student confidentiality.

entries.title: ED School Climate Surveys (EDSCLS)
entries.desc: The ED School Climate Surveys (EDSCLS) provide a suite of nationally available survey instruments developed by NCES to measure school climate from multiple stakeholder perspectives, including students, teachers, non-instructional staff, principals, and parents or guardians. The surveys assess school environment across three domains and 13 topics, with a platform enabling schools, districts, and states to collect and report comparable climate data at the local level. NCES has developed psychometric benchmarks supporting meaningful comparisons across student subgroups and institutions, with scores generated at school, district, and state levels.

entries.title: Education Finance Statistics Center (EDFIN)
entries.desc: The Education Finance Statistics Center (EDFIN) provides financial information and data on public elementary and secondary education in the United States, collecting annual revenues and expenditures data for public schools and school districts alongside fiscal and nonfiscal trend information. The center offers interactive data tools enabling district-level comparisons, the NCES Financial Handbook, and annual publications on education revenues and expenditures by fiscal year. EDFIN serves as the primary federal source for tracking how public education is funded and where resources are allocated across the nation's school districts.

entries.title: Statewide Longitudinal Data Systems (SLDS) Grant Program
entries.desc: The Statewide Longitudinal Data Systems (SLDS) Grant Program supports states in designing, developing, implementing, and expanding K-12 and P-20W (early learning through the workforce) longitudinal data systems that enable evidence-based decision-making across educational levels. The program provides grants, technical assistance, publications, webinars, and a State Support Team to help state education agencies build data infrastructure that tracks student progress from early childhood into the workforce. Key resources include best practices guides, a Research Request Process Toolkit, school-level poverty measures, and connections to federal initiatives such as Common Education Data Standards.

entries.title: Library Statistics Program
entries.desc: The Library Statistics Program collects and distributes statistical data on libraries across the U.S. education system, covering academic libraries, public libraries, state library agencies, and elementary/secondary school library media centers. The academic library component was reintegrated into the IPEDS collection as a mandatory annual survey, with data from 2014 onward accessible through the IPEDS Use the Data portal and historical data from 1996–2012 available through dedicated files. The program maintains historical records extending back to 1989 for public libraries, enabling long-term trend analysis of library resources, staffing, and usage across institutional types.

entries.title: International Activities Program (IAP)
entries.desc: The International Activities Program (IAP) coordinates U.S. participation in large-scale international education studies and assessments to make comparative data available on education and learning across nations. The program administers seven current international studies—including PIRLS, TIMSS, ICILS, PISA, TALIS, PIAAC, and IELS—covering mathematics, science, reading, financial literacy, problem-solving, computer literacy, and adult skills in literacy and numeracy for populations ranging from early childhood through adults aged 16–65. Results typically release 18–24 months after data collection and inform U.S. education policy by contextualizing American student and adult performance within global education systems.

entries.title: Education Across America: Cities, Suburbs, Towns, and Rural Areas (Locale Studies)
entries.desc: The NCES locale studies resource hub, "Education Across America: Cities, Suburbs, Towns, and Rural Areas," compiles comparative data for individuals interested in the condition of education across different geographic locales in the United States. The hub provides tabulations, an interactive "At a Glance" data tool, and indicators spanning family characteristics, educational experiences, school resources and staffing, and educational outcomes across urban, suburban, town, and rural communities. Topics covered include early childhood care, college preparatory coursework, teacher staffing, graduation rates, and postsecondary enrollment, enabling researchers and policymakers to identify geographic disparities in educational opportunity.

entries.title: Current Population Survey (CPS) — Education Supplements
entries.desc: The Current Population Survey (CPS) is a monthly household survey of approximately 50,000–60,000 dwelling units conducted by the Bureau of the Census for the Bureau of Labor Statistics, with NCES funding educational supplements since the late 1960s to provide annual data on school enrollment and educational attainment. The survey's October Supplement gathers school enrollment data across elementary, secondary, and postsecondary levels, plus preschool participation, with supplementary items covering language proficiency, disabilities, computer use and access, student mobility, and private school tuition. The CPS employs a multistage stratified sampling scheme with households rotating in-sample for four months, out for eight, and back for four more, producing nationally representative estimates of the civilian noninstitutionalized population.

entries.title: International Civic and Citizenship Education Study (ICCS)
entries.desc: ICCS is the largest international study dedicated to civic and citizenship education, assessing Grade 8 students' civic knowledge, beliefs, attitudes, and behaviors related to democratic participation. The study also collects contextual data on curriculum, teaching practices, and school climate, with cycles in 2009, 2016, and 2022, and a fully digital 2027 cycle in progress. ICCS data serve as the primary evidence source for UN Sustainable Development Goal indicator 4.7.4 on global citizenship understanding.

entries.title: International Computer and Information Literacy Study (ICILS)
entries.desc: ICILS measures how well Grade 8 students across multiple educational systems are prepared for study, work, and life in a digital world, assessing computer and information literacy (CIL) including the knowledge and skills needed to use technology for investigation, creation, and communication. The study has run in 2013, 2018, and 2023 cycles, with a 2028 cycle currently underway, and supports monitoring of UN SDG targets and EU education priorities. Contextual data on students' home and school technology environments are collected alongside achievement scores.

entries.title: Teacher Education and Development Study in Mathematics (TEDS-M)
entries.desc: TEDS-M examined teacher education programs and their role in developing mathematics instruction capacity, with data collection conducted in 2007-08 across 17 countries. The dataset is maintained by the IEA Research and Analysis Unit and remains publicly available for researcher access upon completion of a nondisclosure and user agreement, given the sensitive nature of participant data. The study is citable via DOI (https://doi.org/10.58150/IEA_TEDS-M_2008) and is actively used in teacher education research; documentation is available through the User Guide for the TEDS-M 2008 International Database.

entries.title: Responses to Educational Disruption Survey (REDS)
entries.desc: REDS examined the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on lower secondary education (Grade 8) across 11 nations including Burkina Faso, Denmark, Ethiopia, India, Kenya, Rwanda, Slovenia, the UAE, Uruguay, and Uzbekistan, with data collected between December 2020 and July 2021. The survey gathered internationally comparable responses from 21,063 students, 15,004 teachers, and 1,581 school principals, enabling cross-national analysis of pandemic-related educational disruption. The dataset is available in SAS and SPSS formats through the IEA Data Repository and is citable via DOI (https://doi.org/10.58150/REDS_2021_data).

entries.title: College Scorecard Data
entries.desc: College Scorecard Data provides downloadable institution-level files for 1996-97 through 2022-23 containing aggregate data on institutional characteristics, enrollment, student aid, costs, and student outcomes, plus field-of-study-level files covering cumulative debt at graduation and earnings one year after graduation. The dataset is updated periodically (last updated March 23, 2026) and also accessible via a public REST API with documentation available on the same site. Crosswalk files link the Department's OPEID identifiers with IPEDS UNITIDs, enabling integration with other federal higher education data sources.

entries.title: Education Stabilization Fund (ESF) COVID-19 Relief Data
entries.desc: The Education Stabilization Fund data portal tracks over $276 billion in federal COVID-19 education relief awarded across four programs: Elementary and Secondary School Emergency Relief (ESSER, $189.5B), Higher Education Emergency Relief (HEER, $75.2B), Emergency Assistance to Non-Public Schools (EANS, $5.5B), and Governor's Emergency Education Relief (GEER, $4.3B). The portal provides grantee-level expenditure data searchable by state, district, and institution, with bulk downloads of annual performance report datasets and a public API. Data covers over 16,000 school districts and 4,500 colleges and universities through the reporting period ending January 2026.

entries.title: ASSISTments 2012-13 School Data with Affect
entries.desc: A large-scale log of approximately 10 million student problem-solving interactions from the ASSISTments intelligent tutoring system during the 2012-2013 school year, augmented with machine-learning predictions of four affective states per interaction — frustration, confusion, engagement/concentration, and boredom — scored on a 0-to-1 scale. Records include student attempts, hint requests, response times, problem metadata, skill tags, and classroom/school identifiers, enabling research on the relationship between student affect and math learning outcomes in K-12 settings.

entries.title: ASSISTments Skill-Builder Data 2009-2010
entries.desc: Student interaction logs from ASSISTments skill-builder problem sets used in K-12 mathematics during the 2009-2010 school year, structured as one row per student-problem attempt. Each skill-builder focuses on a single mathematics skill (with optional multi-skill tagging) and requires students to answer three consecutive questions correctly before moving on; the dataset captures correctness, hint usage, and response sequences under this mastery criterion. A corrected, deduplicated version is available via Google Drive alongside the original CSV.

entries.title: Literacy and Numeracy Assessment (LaNA)
entries.desc: LaNA is an international assessment of emerging mathematics and reading comprehension skills at the end of primary school (grades 4–6 depending on context), designed for countries that need timely, reliable, nationally representative, and internationally comparable education data. It is grounded in and linked to TIMSS and PIRLS, using the same benchmarks to track progress toward SDG Indicator 4.1.1b. The study collects contextual information about resources, learning experiences, and school environments alongside achievement data. Six countries have administered LaNA: Burkina Faso, Egypt, Nigeria, Pakistan, Palestinian National Authority, and Senegal, with earlier pilots in Haiti, North Macedonia, Punjab (Pakistan), and Serbia. Initial administrations occurred in 2016–2020 (pilots) and 2023 (linking study).

entries.title: TIMSS Advanced
entries.desc: TIMSS Advanced assesses students in their final year of secondary school who are enrolled in advanced mathematics and physics programs. It is the only international assessment measuring student readiness for university-level STEM studies, gathering policy-relevant data on curriculum emphasis, technology use, and teacher preparation to support education reform and STEM policy planning. Data from three cycles are available through the IEA repository in SAS and SPSS formats: 1995, 2008, and 2015. Each dataset is assigned a DOI for citation purposes.

entries.title: TIMSS Longitudinal
entries.desc: TIMSS Longitudinal is an optional extension of TIMSS 2023 that measures student learning gains over one year of schooling by assessing the same cohort at two points in time. It targets students in grades 4–5 and grades 8–9, capturing growth in mathematics and science achievement across a critical schooling period. Data from the 2023 and 2024 collections are publicly available in SAS, SPSS, and R formats through the IEA data repository, with DOI citations provided for academic use.

entries.title: CIVED — Civic Education Study
entries.desc: CIVED was an international assessment covering the content domains of democracy and citizenship, national identity, and social cohesion and diversity. Described as the largest and most rigorous study of civic education conducted internationally at the time, it assessed students at grades 8 and 12. Phase 1 of the study ran from 1996–1997 and Phase 2 from 1999–2000. Datasets for the 1999 assessment cycles at both grade levels are publicly available in SAS and SPSS formats through the IEA data repository. CIVED is the predecessor to ICCS, which continues civic education measurement in subsequent cycles.

entries.title: IELS — International Early Learning and Child Well-being Study
entries.desc: IELS is the first international large-scale assessment focused on five-year-old children, examining early learning, home background and learning environments, and experiences in early childhood education. It measures both cognitive domains (emergent numeracy and literacy) and non-cognitive domains (self-regulation and empathy) through tablet-based one-to-one sessions with audio recordings. The first cycle was completed in 2018 in three countries; a second cycle, IELS 2024, is currently underway. The study is coordinated by the OECD Secretariat with a consortium led by the Australian Council for Educational Research, including IEA and cApStAn as partners.

entries.title: Duolingo Student Learning Traces (Spaced Repetition / Half-Life Regression)
entries.desc: A dataset of 13 million student learning traces from Duolingo, capturing vocabulary practice sessions across multiple language pairs. Released alongside the paper "A Trainable Spaced Repetition Model for Language Learning" (Settles & Meeder, ACL 2016), which introduced the half-life regression algorithm for modeling memory decay. Each record represents a single lesson or practice session and includes recall accuracy, timing, student and language identifiers, and word-level history of exposures and correct answers (12 columns total). The dataset is available as a 361 MB gzipped CSV and is hosted on Harvard Dataverse. Code is available at https://github.com/duolingo/halflife-regression. Descriptions sourced from research.duolingo.com and the GitHub repository README.

entries.title: STEP Skills Measurement Program
entries.desc: The STEP (Skills Toward Employment and Productivity) Skills Measurement Program is the World Bank's first initiative to generate internationally comparable data on workforce skills in low- and middle-income countries. The program administers household and employer surveys across 17 countries — including Armenia, Bolivia, Colombia, Georgia, Ghana, Kenya, and Vietnam — measuring cognitive skills (reading, writing, numeracy), socio-emotional competencies, and job-specific skills, along with education and training histories. The collection of 22 studies is publicly available through the World Bank Microdata Library, with each country survey including a direct reading literacy assessment based on the Programme for the International Assessment of Adult Competencies (PIAAC) instruments.

entries.title: Service Delivery Indicators Education Survey 2014 — Tanzania
entries.desc: This World Bank dataset examines primary education service delivery quality across Tanzania through a 2014 survey of 400 primary schools. It captures three standardized indicator categories: teacher effort (school and classroom absence rates, daily teaching time), teacher knowledge (subject matter competency in English, mathematics, and pedagogy), and availability of inputs (infrastructure, textbooks, equipment, and pupil-teacher ratios). The dataset also includes direct grade-4 student assessments in language, mathematics, and non-verbal reasoning, and is harmonized to enable cross-country comparisons within the broader Service Delivery Indicators initiative covering Sub-Saharan Africa.

entries.title: National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent to Adult Health (Add Health), 1994–2025
entries.desc: Add Health is a nationally representative longitudinal panel study tracking a cohort of U.S. adolescents from grades 7–12 (1994–95) through early midlife, with data collection across six waves extending to 2025 when participants were ages 39–51. The study combines survey data on social, economic, psychological, and physical well-being with rich contextual information on families, schools, neighborhoods, and peer and romantic relationships; later waves add biospecimens, physical measurements, and comprehensive life history data. It is one of the largest and most widely cited longitudinal datasets in U.S. education and adolescent development research, supporting studies of school transitions, educational attainment, and long-term health and socioeconomic outcomes.

entries.title: WPI — ASSISTments Math 2004–2005 (912 Students)
entries.desc: A public dataset in the PSLC DataShop repository containing interaction logs from 912 students using the ASSISTments intelligent tutoring system during the 2004–2005 academic year. The dataset, contributed by PI Neil Heffernan (Worcester Polytechnic Institute), records student problem-solving transactions in middle-school mathematics. It is one of three ASSISTments datasets in DataShop spanning 2004–2007, with this cohort containing approximately 580,785 transactions. Part of the WPI–ASSISTments project (DataShop ID 92), listed as a public project accessible to registered DataShop users.

entries.title: WPI — ASSISTments Math 2005–2006 (3136 Students)
entries.desc: A public PSLC DataShop dataset capturing 3,136 students' interactions with the ASSISTments intelligent tutoring platform during the 2005–2006 school year, contributed by PI Neil Heffernan. ASSISTments provides students with scaffolded math problem-solving practice while simultaneously collecting fine-grained transaction-level data (student responses, hints requested, errors made) suitable for knowledge component modeling and learning curve analysis. This dataset (DataShop ID 120) contains approximately 1,451,003 transactions — the largest of the three ASSISTments cohorts in DataShop. Listed as a public project on the DataShop catalog.

entries.title: Geometry Area (1996–97)
entries.desc: One of the oldest and most widely used benchmark datasets in educational data mining, this public PSLC DataShop dataset records students' interactions with a Geometry Area Cognitive Tutor during the 1996–97 academic year. PI Ken Koedinger (CMU). The dataset has been assigned a persistent DOI (10.60710/DATASET-76) as part of DataShop's April 2026 DOI release. It has been used in numerous learning science and EDM studies and was featured as the standard teaching dataset for the EDM Summer School 2017 (DataShop ID 2174). Subject area: Math/Geometry. Listed as a public project.

entries.title: Physics Course — Andes2 (USNA, 2005–2010)
entries.desc: A public collection of 15 datasets capturing physics students' problem-solving interactions with the Andes2 intelligent tutoring system, contributed by PI Kurt VanLehn. Data spans multiple institutions and semesters from 2001 to 2010, primarily at the United States Naval Academy (USNA) but also including Watchung Hills Regional High School, St. Anselm College, and others. Individual datasets range from roughly 10,000 to 345,000 transactions. Andes is a physics tutor that provides step-by-step problem-solving support with hints and error feedback. The dataset collection (DataShop IDs 126, 157, 220, 233–238, 263, 265, 267, 302–304, 408–412) is listed as a public project on DataShop.

entries.title: Junyi Academy Math Practicing Log (to January 2015)
entries.desc: A public PSLC DataShop dataset contributed by PI Haw-Shiuan Chang capturing student math practice logs from Junyi Academy, a Taiwanese e-learning platform described as functionally similar to Khan Academy. The dataset records student interactions with mathematics exercises through January 2015 and is available in full (DataShop ID 1198) and partial-sample (DataShop ID 1275) versions. Subject area: Math/Other. Listed as a public project, this large-scale log dataset from a deployed online platform is suited for studies of learning trajectories, knowledge component modeling, and engagement in self-paced digital learning environments.

entries.title: Adapterrex — Erroneous Examples Studies (2011–2019)
entries.desc: A public collection of 10 datasets from the Adapterrex project, contributed by PI Bruce McLaren (CMU), investigating the learning benefits of erroneous examples versus conventional problem-solving in middle-school mathematics. Studies ran from Fall 2011 through Spring 2019 across multiple school sites (Hopewell, Steel Valley, and others), with transaction counts ranging from approximately 4,300 to 376,000 per dataset. The research used randomized controlled designs (ErrEx vs. PS conditions) to examine whether studying worked examples containing deliberate errors improves conceptual understanding and transfer. DataShop IDs 495, 534, 579, 586, 592, 659, 1307, 2223, 2648, 2996. Listed as a public project.

entries.title: Blocked vs. Interleaved Instruction — Fraction Arithmetic
entries.desc: A public PSLC DataShop dataset contributed by PI Rony Patel examining the effects of blocked versus interleaved practice on fraction arithmetic (addition and multiplication) learning. Two datasets are available: DS1190 (Fraction Addition and Multiplication, April 2015–August 2016, ~24,453 transactions) and DS1706 (Blocked vs. Interleaved condition comparison, August 2016–March 2018, ~365,312 transactions). Subject area: Math/Other. The datasets support analysis of how the sequencing of different problem types during practice affects learning outcomes, a topic with strong relevance to instructional design and spacing/interleaving research.

entries.title: iSnap — Introductory Programming (2015–2018)
entries.desc: A public collection of seven datasets capturing students' block-based programming interactions with iSnap, a logging-enabled fork of the Snap! visual programming environment, contributed by PI Thomas Price (NC State University). Data spans Fall 2015 through Spring 2018 (DataShop IDs 1920, 1921, 1922, 2517, 2724, 2738, 5667) with per-semester transaction counts ranging from approximately 84,000 to 208,000. iSnap records detailed step-by-step programming actions — code edits, hint requests, and project states — enabling fine-grained analysis of novice programming behavior, hint-seeking strategies, and knowledge component modeling for introductory CS education. Listed as a public project on DataShop.

entries.title: Virginia Tech OpenDSA — Formal Languages eTextbook (Fall 2019)
entries.desc: A public PSLC DataShop dataset contributed by PI Cliff Shaffer (Virginia Tech) recording student engagement with the OpenDSA open-source interactive eTextbook covering Formal Languages during Fall 2019. OpenDSA delivers algorithm and data-structures content through embedded visualizations, exercises, and automated feedback. The dataset captures student interaction logs from a deployed university course, supporting research on self-paced eTextbook usage, exercise completion patterns, and learning from interactive educational resources. Subject area: Computer Science/Other. Listed as a public project (DataShop ID 3427).

entries.title: Pavlik — Knowledge Tracking: Chinese Vocabulary Studies (2006–2007)
entries.desc: A public collection of eight datasets from the Pavlik Knowledge Tracking project, contributed by PI Phil Pavlik (University of Memphis, formerly CMU), capturing students' Chinese vocabulary and character learning in computer-assisted language practice sessions. Datasets span Spring 2006 through Fall 2007 and include studies of vocabulary scheduling, Chinese radical transfer, and pinyin learning (DataShop IDs 107, 108, 115, 160, 161, 212, 213, 2745). Subject areas: Language/Chinese, Language/Other. Transaction counts range from tens of thousands to over 100,000 per dataset. The studies informed the development of the ACT-R–based spacing algorithm used in Pavlik's knowledge tracing and memory models. Listed as a public project on DataShop.

entries.title: Education at a Glance (EAG)
entries.desc: Education at a Glance is OECD's authoritative annual statistical compendium on education systems worldwide, published each September. Each edition provides more than 100 internationally harmonised indicators covering the output of educational institutions; the impact of learning on earnings and employment; access, participation, and progression through education levels; financial resources invested in education; and teachers, learning environments, and school organisation. Data are drawn from the joint UNESCO-OECD-Eurostat (UOE) administrative data collection and from OECD's own surveys (PISA, TALIS, PIAAC), and cover OECD member countries plus a range of partner economies. The 2025 edition (released September 2025, 542 pages) is the most recent. Researchers use EAG as the primary cross-national reference for structural and system-level education indicators.

entries.title: PISA for Development (PISA-D)
entries.desc: PISA for Development (PISA-D) is an adapted version of the PISA assessment designed to support low- and middle-income countries in using internationally comparable assessment data to monitor progress toward national targets and SDG 4. PISA-D uses the same proficiency scales as standard PISA but adds more granular performance descriptors at the lower end of the scale, enhanced questionnaire instruments relevant to developing-country contexts, and — uniquely — an out-of-school component assessing 14–16-year-olds who are not enrolled. Nine countries participated in the school-based assessment (Bhutan, Cambodia, Ecuador, Guatemala, Honduras, Paraguay, Senegal, Zambia); five conducted the out-of-school assessment (Guatemala, Honduras, Panama, Paraguay, Senegal). School-based results were released December 2018; out-of-school results in 2020. Public-use files in SAS and SPSS formats are freely downloadable and include student, principal, teacher, parent, and out-of-school respondent records, codebooks, questionnaires, and data compendia.

entries.title: TALIS Starting Strong
entries.desc: TALIS Starting Strong is an international large-scale survey of staff and leaders in early childhood education and care (ECEC), conducted under the OECD Teaching and Learning International Survey programme. Its goal is to generate robust internationally comparable data on the ECEC workforce to inform policy on staff working conditions, professional development, pedagogical practices and beliefs, well-being, job satisfaction, and intent to remain in the profession. The survey also addresses diversity in ECEC settings, use of digital technologies, and leadership practices. The 2024 cycle collected data from approximately 17 countries and subnational entities; an earlier 2018 cycle is also available. Public-use files in CSV, Rds, and SPSS formats are downloadable for both cycles, alongside questionnaires, conceptual frameworks, technical reports, and user guides. The dataset is closely related to TALIS (which covers primary and secondary teachers) but focuses exclusively on ECEC settings.

entries.title: Survey on Social and Emotional Skills (SSES)
entries.desc: The OECD Survey on Social and Emotional Skills (SSES) is the largest global initiative to gather comparable cross-national data on the social and emotional skill development of 10- and 15-year-old students. The survey measures a broad set of skills organised into five domains: task performance (persistence, responsibility, self-control, achievement motivation), emotional regulation (stress resistance, emotional control, optimism), engaging with others (assertiveness, sociability, energy), open-mindedness (curiosity, creativity, tolerance), and collaboration. Contextual data are collected from students, teachers, school principals, and (optionally) parents. Round 1 (2019) covered 10 cities/regions; Round 2 (2023) expanded to 16 sites across six countries and ten sub-national entities — including Bulgaria, Chile, Peru, Spain, Mexico, Ukraine, and cities in Colombia, India, UAE, Italy, Japan, Finland, China, Indonesia, and Brazil — surveying approximately 18,000 ten-year-olds and 52,000 fifteen-year-olds. Datasets for both rounds are available on request from OECD. The SSES 2023 database page and 2019 database page provide application and access details.

entries.title: OECD Online Education Database (UOE)
entries.desc: The OECD Online Education Database is the annual administrative data collection underpinning Education at a Glance indicators. Conducted jointly by OECD, the UNESCO Institute for Statistics, and Eurostat (the UOE joint data collection), it has run in its current form since 1993. The collection comprises approximately 25 electronic questionnaires covering student enrolments (nine questionnaires), student entrants (four), graduates (three), personnel (three), education finance (three), class sizes (two), and general population data (one). Data are reported by Ministries of Education or national statistical offices according to international standards and the ISCED classification. Indicators derived from UOE cover educational outputs, access, participation and progression, human and financial resources invested in education, and structural features of national education systems. Raw UOE data are available through the OECD Education data portal and through the OECD.Stat platform; methodological guidance is provided in the UOE Data Collection Manual.

entries.title: IDEA Section 618 Data
entries.desc: The IDEA Section 618 Data portal collects and publishes annual data on children and youth with disabilities receiving federally mandated special education services under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA). Coverage spans infants and toddlers (birth through age 2) served under Part C early intervention programs, and children ages 3–21 served under Part B special education programs. A searchable database allows users to retrieve state-submitted datasets by year and category; additional data notes and downloadable products are linked to ED's Open Data Platform at data.ed.gov.

entries.title: National Training, Education, and Workforce Survey (NTEWS)
entries.desc: NTEWS is a biennial household survey co-sponsored by NCES and the National Science Foundation's National Center for Science and Engineering Statistics (NCSES), administered by the U.S. Census Bureau. It collects data on educational enrollment and attainment, nondegree credential types, work experience programs (apprenticeships and internships), employment characteristics, and demographic characteristics, with particular emphasis on the Skilled Technical Workforce — individuals with advanced science and engineering skills but no bachelor's degree. The pilot phase launched in 2022 targeting adults ages 16–75, with the next collection planned for spring 2024; pilot estimates are designated experimental statistics pending quality review.

entries.title: Condition of Education
entries.desc: The Condition of Education is NCES's flagship annual report presenting key education indicators across all levels of the U.S. system, from early childhood through postsecondary education and into the labor market. The 2024 edition covers six major topic areas — family characteristics, elementary and secondary education, postsecondary enrollment and outcomes, employment and earnings by educational attainment, international comparisons, and school crime and safety — with data updated throughout the year as new sources become available. Indicators are accessible as interactive web pages organized by topic and demographic subgroup, with PDF archives dating back to 1989.

entries.title: Digest of Education Statistics
entries.desc: The Digest of Education Statistics is an annual NCES compendium spanning the full breadth of American education from prekindergarten through graduate school. It aggregates data from both government and private sources on schools, colleges, teachers, enrollments, graduates, educational attainment, finances, and federal funding, supplemented by population trends, labor force characteristics, and international comparisons. Current tables and historical versions are available online back to 1995, with PDF archives from 1990–2020; a new Digest State Dashboard provides state-level interactive profiles for all 50 states, D.C., territories, and the Bureau of Indian Education.

entries.title: Education Recovery Project — COVID Learning Loss Data
entries.desc: A national dataset tracking pandemic-related learning loss and recovery across U.S. school districts in reading and math from 2019 to 2024, released by Stanford's Educational Opportunity Project (SEDA 2024.2, updated February 2026). As of spring 2024, U.S. students remained nearly half a grade level behind pre-pandemic achievement in both subjects; the data also captures the impact of remote learning policies and federal pandemic relief funding on district-level recovery trajectories. Download requires a data use agreement; raw files are available for independent analysis alongside an interactive district-level explorer.

entries.title: Segregation Tracking Project — School and Neighborhood Segregation Data
entries.desc: A multi-decade dataset tracking racial/ethnic and economic segregation across U.S. schools and neighborhoods, available at the state, county, metropolitan area, and school-district level (version 2.0, updated February 2026). Underlying research analyzes patterns from 1991 to 2020 and finds that school segregation has increased over the past three decades, particularly in large urban districts, driven substantially by policy choices. Raw data are downloadable under a data use agreement alongside an interactive mapping explorer and peer-reviewed methods documentation.

entries.title: District Court Order Desegregation Data
entries.desc: A dataset cataloging all medium-to-large U.S. school districts ever placed under a court-ordered desegregation plan, covering 358 court cases affecting 850 school districts plus 207 HEW actions since 1978. Each record includes case name, state, NCES district ID, year of initial case, current plan status, and dismissal date; data were compiled from case dockets, Department of Justice records, NAACP Legal Defense materials, and U.S. Department of Education files and verified against Westlaw. Available in Stata (versions 12 and 15), Excel, SAS 7, and SPSS formats; last updated February 2021 with revised dismissal statuses.

entries.title: Opportunity Atlas — Neighborhood-Level Economic Mobility Data
entries.desc: A publicly available dataset built from anonymized longitudinal records covering nearly the entire U.S. population, tracking outcomes for children from the 1984–1989 birth cohorts (measured at approximately age 35) by Census tract, county, commuting zone, and national level. Measures include household income, incarceration rates, and neighborhood characteristics, all stratified by parental income percentile, race, and gender. Data files are downloadable in Stata and Excel formats from the Opportunity Insights website, with a full replication package on Harvard Dataverse (DOI: 10.7910/DVN/NALG3E) and an interactive tool at opportunityatlas.org.

entries.title: Mobility Report Cards — College-Level Economic Mobility Data
entries.desc: A comprehensive college-level dataset linking anonymized federal administrative records to measure income mobility at U.S. colleges, including students' earnings in their thirties, parents' income levels, ACT/SAT scores, and IPEDS/College Scorecard institution characteristics across multiple birth cohorts. The data enable analysis of which colleges function as engines of economic mobility and at what rates, enabling comparisons of mobility rates and access patterns by income quintile. Individual data tables (15 in total) are available in Stata and Excel formats, with a full replication package on Harvard Dataverse; an interactive New York Times visualization also accompanies the release.

entries.title: College-Level Data for 139 Selective American Colleges
entries.desc: A dataset covering 139 selective U.S. colleges, linking anonymized admissions records with income tax data and SAT/ACT scores to analyze the determinants of admission and the causal effects of attendance on socioeconomic outcomes. The data support analysis of how parental income influences admission rates at elite institutions and the downstream earnings consequences of attending selective versus non-selective colleges. Freely available in CSV and Stata formats with an accompanying codebook; an interactive New York Times visualization ("How Income Influences College Attendance") accompanies the release.

entries.title: Social Capital Atlas — County, ZIP, High School, and College Data
entries.desc: A dataset measuring three dimensions of social capital — economic connectedness between high- and low-SES individuals, social cohesion (clique formation in friendship networks), and civic engagement (volunteering rates) — derived from analysis of 21 billion Facebook friendships. Data are available at the county, ZIP code, high school, and college levels, including a 100×100 friending-rate matrix across socioeconomic groups, enabling research on which communities provide cross-class social ties that support upward income mobility. All files are freely downloadable in CSV format from opportunityinsights.org/data/ with an interactive Social Capital Atlas at socialcapital.org.

entries.title: Who Becomes an Inventor — Innovation Rates by Childhood Location and Demographics
entries.desc: A dataset tracking more than one million U.S. inventors by linking patent records to tax records and school district records, providing innovation rates organized by childhood commuting zone and state, gender, parental income quintile, educational institution attended, and career trajectory including income distributions. The data reveal that children from high-income families are significantly more likely to become inventors, largely due to exposure to innovation rather than innate ability — the so-called "lost Einsteins" phenomenon. Available in Stata, Excel, and README formats as individual tables and a bundled ZIP, with a full replication package on Harvard Dataverse.

entries.title: Race and Economic Opportunity — Intergenerational Mobility by Race
entries.desc: A dataset derived from de-identified longitudinal records covering nearly the entire U.S. population from 1989 to 2015, measuring intergenerational income mobility and related outcomes disaggregated by race, gender, and parental income percentile at the national, commuting zone, and county levels. Data tables include national statistics by parent income percentile and race, income transition matrices, and estimates for second-generation immigrants by country of origin, as well as intergenerational educational attainment matrices. Files are downloadable in Stata and Excel formats; a full replication package is available on GitHub and Harvard Dataverse.

entries.title: Changing Opportunity — County and Commuting Zone Trend Data (1978–1992 Cohorts)
entries.desc: A dataset covering birth cohorts from 1978 to 1992 at the county, commuting zone, and national levels, tracking trends in earnings, educational attainment, standardized test scores, and mortality rates by parental income, race, and gender over time. The data document growing class gaps and shrinking race gaps in economic opportunity across these cohorts and include a crosswalk converting income percentiles to 2023 dollars. All tables are freely downloadable in CSV and Stata formats with PDF codebooks from the Opportunity Insights website.

entries.title: Geography of Intergenerational Mobility — Commuting Zone and County Data
entries.desc: A foundational dataset analyzing intergenerational income mobility using administrative records from more than 40 million children and their parents, covering commuting zones, counties, and metropolitan statistical areas across the United States. In addition to mobility statistics and income transition matrices, the dataset includes community characteristics correlated with upward mobility — school quality, segregation levels, income inequality, social capital, and family stability — supporting causal analysis of place effects on children's outcomes. Data are available in Excel and Stata formats (10 online tables) with a bundled replication package; full dataset on Harvard Dataverse (DOI: 10.7910/DVN/NALG3E).

entries.title: NSC High School Benchmarks — Postsecondary Outcomes Data Appendix
entries.desc: An annually updated Excel data appendix accompanying the NSC High School Benchmarks report (13th edition in 2025), providing postsecondary enrollment, first-year persistence, and six-year credential completion rates for three high school graduating classes (2024, 2022, and 2018 respectively). Outcomes are disaggregated by high school characteristics including income level, minority enrollment percentage, and geographic locale, enabling comparisons between high- and low-poverty schools; for example, STEM completion rates ranged from 8.1% at high-poverty schools to 22.4% at low-poverty schools for the class of 2018. The 2025 data appendix is directly downloadable as an XLSX file from the report page, with prior-year appendices available back to at least 2022.

entries.title: NSC Some College, No Credential — National and State Outcomes Data
entries.desc: Downloadable Excel appendices from the NSC Some College, No Credential (SCNC) annual report series, providing national and state-level data on the tens of millions of U.S. adults who left higher education without a postsecondary credential. The National/State Data appendix covers demographic profiles and state SCNC populations and outcomes; the National Outcomes Data appendix tracks re-enrollment rates, credential completion, and persistence across states and institutional sectors. Annual data are available for 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025 in XLSX format, supplemented by interactive Tableau dashboards; historical reports extend back to 2014.

entries.title: Urban Institute Education Data Portal — API Documentation and Data Sources
entries.desc: The full API documentation for the Urban Institute's Education Data Portal, which harmonizes and provides unified programmatic access to six major federal education datasets: the Common Core of Data (CCD) school directory and enrollment data, the Civil Rights Data Collection, the Small Area Income and Poverty Estimates, IPEDS postsecondary institution data, the College Scorecard, and the National Historical Geographic Information System (NHGIS). The API supports queries by education level (schools, districts, colleges), geography, time frame, and indicator; code interfaces are available for Stata, R, Python, and JavaScript. Data are openly accessible without authentication for most endpoints; the documentation page lists all currently available endpoints and variables.

entries.title: Current Population Survey (CPS) Data at NBER
entries.desc: The NBER's maintained archive of the U.S. Current Population Survey, the federal government's primary monthly survey of labor force participation and employment, spanning basic monthly data from 1976 to the present and CPS supplements from 1964 onward. The archive includes the Merged Outgoing Rotation Groups (MORG) files for earnings analysis, the Mare-Winship standardized subsets (1964–1992), and thematic supplement extracts covering schooling, fertility, immigration, and income — making it a key source for longitudinal analysis of educational attainment and its labor market returns. All files are freely downloadable from the NBER website with SAS, SPSS, and Stata data definition files and comprehensive methodology documentation.
